class_schedule.topic: The Digital Marketing Landscape and Strategic Framework
learning_objectives: Define digital marketing and distinguish it from traditional marketing in terms of measurability, targeting, and interactivity
Identify the major channels that compose the digital marketing ecosystem: owned, earned, and paid media
Apply the RACE (Reach, Act, Convert, Engage) planning framework to a real brand's customer journey
Analyze how digital transformation has reshaped consumer expectations and brand-customer relationships
readings: Chaffey & Ellis-Chadwick, Digital Marketing: Strategy, Implementation and Practice (8th ed.), Ch. 1 - Introducing Digital Marketing
Kotler, Kartajaya & Setiawan, Marketing 5.0: Technology for Humanity, Ch. 1-2
Harvard Business Review, 'The New Rules of Marketing Strategy in the Digital Age' (2023)
Smart Insights, 'The RACE Planning Framework' - reference document

lecture_notes.introduction: Digital marketing has fundamentally reshaped how brands discover, acquire, convert, and retain customers. In 2024, global digital ad spending exceeded 740 billion dollars, surpassing traditional media for the seventh consecutive year, and marketers who cannot operate in digital channels are essentially operating with one hand tied behind their back. Consider Nike: when they terminated partnerships with Foot Locker, Amazon, and other wholesalers between 2020 and 2022 to go direct-to-consumer, their entire business model depended on digital marketing capability. Their revenue from the Nike app, SNKRS app, and Nike.com had to replace billions in wholesale revenue, which required mastery of SEO, paid social, email, loyalty, and mobile commerce working in concert. When execution fell short in 2024, the company lost an estimated 28 percent of market capitalization in a single year, forcing a CEO change. Digital marketing is not a separate discipline anymore; it is marketing. This course will take you from the strategic framework through hands-on tactical execution, teaching you to plan campaigns, execute them across channels, measure their impact with analytics, and optimize them based on evidence rather than intuition. By the end, you will be able to design and defend a complete digital marketing strategy for a real brand, backed by data.
key_concepts: Digital marketing definition: the use of digital channels, devices, and platforms to promote products and services, build brand awareness, acquire customers, and nurture relationships; characterized by measurability, targeting precision, interactivity, and real-time optimization
Owned media: channels a brand controls directly (website, mobile app, email list, physical stores); the foundation of digital marketing because you own the audience relationship and data
Earned media: exposure gained through third-party endorsement (press coverage, customer reviews, organic social shares, word of mouth); powerful but not directly controllable
Paid media: channels where the brand pays for reach (Google Ads, Meta Ads, display advertising, sponsored content, influencer partnerships); predictable but expensive and stops when spending stops

RACE planning framework (Smart Insights): Reach (attract audiences via SEO, content, paid ads), Act (engage through website interactions and micro-conversions), Convert (turn prospects into customers), Engage (retain and grow customer lifetime value)
Digital marketing funnel: awareness (top of funnel), consideration (middle), purchase (bottom), retention and advocacy (beyond purchase); each stage requires different content, channels, and metrics

The fundamental shift from interruption to permission marketing: traditional advertising interrupts unrelated content, while digital marketing increasingly requires earning attention through value, relevance, and consent (email opt-ins, app installs, follows)

Consumer digital behavior: the average consumer touches 8 or more digital touchpoints before purchase (Google research); these touchpoints span search, social, video, review sites, and branded properties

Data-driven decision making: unlike traditional marketing where measurement was limited to surveys and brand tracking, digital enables attribution to individual users and actions, enabling rapid testing and optimization

The D2C disruption: digitally native vertical brands (Warby Parker, Dollar Shave Club, Glossier, Casper) proved that digital-first strategies could dismantle established retail categories by owning the direct customer relationship
lecture_notes.demonstration: Case study analysis: map Glossier's customer journey through the RACE framework. Reach: Instagram content showcasing user-generated content and tutorial videos attracts prospects searching for skincare and makeup inspiration. Act: Landing pages designed around editorial storytelling and customer reviews keep visitors engaged; email signup incentives convert traffic to leads. Convert: Seamless mobile checkout with Apple Pay and Shop Pay reduces friction; abandoned cart emails recover 15-25 percent of lost sales. Engage: Into the Gloss blog and community forums keep customers engaged post-purchase; loyalty tiers and early access drive repeat purchases. Calculate back-of-envelope metrics: if Glossier reaches 5 million people monthly through Instagram with a 2 percent click-through to website, converts 3 percent of visitors to customers at $40 average order value, what is monthly revenue from this channel alone? Answer: 5M x 0.02 x 0.03 x $40 = $120,000 per month, extrapolated to $1.44M annually from a single channel.
setup_requirements: Google account for accessing Google Analytics Demo account and Google Trends
Smart Insights RACE framework template (PDF download)
SimilarWeb free tier account for analyzing brand traffic sources
Spreadsheet software (Google Sheets or Excel) for journey mapping exercise

review.value: The distinction between 'digital marketing' and 'traditional marketing' is increasingly meaningless; every modern marketer must be digitally capable. Nike's D2C pivot demonstrates that even the largest heritage brands now depend on digital execution for billions in revenue

Owned media (email list, website, app) is strategically the most valuable because you control the relationship and the data; earned media is free but unpredictable; paid media is reliable but expensive and evaporates when spending stops. Build owned media aggressively

When a brand's performance is underwhelming, the RACE framework helps diagnose WHERE the problem lives: insufficient Reach (no one knows you), weak Act (visitors bounce without engaging), poor Convert (engagement does not become sales), or no Engage (customers do not return)

The ability to measure everything in digital does not automatically produce insight; marketers who build disciplined measurement frameworks, avoid vanity metrics, and connect spend to revenue have a durable advantage over those who chase impressions

class_schedule.topic: Consumer Behavior Online and the Digital Customer Journey
learning_objectives: Map the modern multi-touchpoint customer journey from awareness through advocacy across devices and channels
Identify the key moments of truth (ZMOT, FMOT, SMOT) in a digital buying process
Analyze how search intent (informational, navigational, commercial, transactional) shapes keyword strategy
Apply customer persona development and journey mapping techniques to a specific brand scenario
readings: Chaffey & Ellis-Chadwick, Digital Marketing (8th ed.), Ch. 2 - Online Marketplace Analysis
Google, 'The Zero Moment of Truth' research report (Jim Lecinski)
Think with Google, 'Micro-Moments: Your Guide to Winning the Shift to Mobile'
Kotler, Kartajaya & Setiawan, Marketing 5.0, Ch. 4 - The New Customer Experience

lecture_notes.introduction: Understanding how real people actually behave online is the foundation of every effective digital marketing campaign. The days of a linear AIDA funnel (Attention, Interest, Desire, Action) are long gone; today's journey is messy, non-linear, multi-device, and often spans weeks or months before a purchase decision. When a consumer decides to buy a new pair of running shoes, they may start with a Google search ('best running shoes for flat feet'), watch YouTube comparison videos, read Runner's World reviews, check Reddit subreddits for unfiltered opinions, look up Instagram influencer posts, visit the brand website three times over two weeks, abandon a cart once, return after seeing a retargeting ad, and finally buy in a physical store after trying them on. Every one of those touchpoints is an opportunity for the brand to influence the decision, or to lose it. Google's research into the 'Zero Moment of Truth' (ZMOT) found that the average consumer consults 10.4 sources before making a purchase decision, up from 5.3 just a few years prior. For high-consideration purchases like cars or mortgages, that number can exceed 20. This session teaches you to see the world through the customer's eyes, map their real journey, identify the moments that matter, and design marketing that shows up in the right place at the right time with the right message. Getting this wrong means spending budget on channels your customers do not use, or showing up with messaging that does not match their mindset at that stage.
key_concepts: The Zero Moment of Truth (ZMOT): the moment when a consumer researches a product online BEFORE talking to a salesperson or seeing the product in store; Google identified this as the decisive moment for most purchases

First Moment of Truth (FMOT): the in-store or at-product moment when consumer encounters the physical product; Second Moment of Truth (SMOT): the experience of using the product after purchase, which shapes loyalty and reviews

Micro-moments (Google framework): I-want-to-know moments (research), I-want-to-go moments (local intent), I-want-to-do moments (how-to), I-want-to-buy moments (purchase intent); brands that show up in the right micro-moment win the customer

Search intent categories: informational (learning about a topic), navigational (finding a specific site), commercial investigation (comparing options), transactional (ready to buy); each intent requires different content and keyword strategies
Multi-device journeys: a customer may research on mobile during lunch, compare on desktop at work, and purchase on tablet at home; cross-device tracking and consistent experiences are critical

The non-linear journey: modern customers loop back, jump between stages, and consult multiple brands simultaneously; Google's 'messy middle' model replaces the linear funnel with exploration and evaluation loops

Customer personas: semi-fictional representations of ideal customers based on real data about demographics, behaviors, motivations, and goals; well-built personas inform every creative and channel decision

Journey mapping: a visual representation of the customer experience across touchpoints, emotions, pain points, and opportunities; typically structured as stages (awareness, consideration, decision, retention, advocacy) vs touchpoints
Touchpoint inventory: cataloging every interaction a customer has with the brand across channels and over time; the foundation for identifying gaps and optimization opportunities
Jobs-to-be-Done framework: customers 'hire' products to accomplish specific jobs; understanding the job (not just demographics) reveals why people choose or reject your product
lecture_notes.demonstration: Build a customer journey map for Dollar Shave Club targeting a 28-year-old male professional who currently buys razors at Target. Stages: (1) Trigger: sees a viral YouTube ad from a friend's share. (2) Research: Googles 'Dollar Shave Club review' and reads blog and Reddit comparisons with Harry's and Gillette. (3) Evaluation: visits dollarshaveclub.com, explores plans, watches explainer videos. (4) Conversion: signs up for $5 starter kit, provides credit card. (5) Fulfillment: receives box 3-5 days later, opens branded unboxing experience. (6) Usage: uses razors for a month, receives replenishment automatically. (7) Advocacy: posts about it on Instagram or shares referral link. For each stage, identify: customer emotions, touchpoints, content needs, potential failure points, and metrics. For example, at the research stage, emotion is curious-skeptical, touchpoints are Google SERPs, review sites, and social media, and the key metric is time-on-site and pages-per-visit for content consumption.
setup_requirements: Miro or Lucidchart account for journey mapping
Google Trends and Google Keyword Planner access for search intent analysis
Customer persona template (HubSpot or Buyer Persona Institute format)
Access to one brand's website and social channels for analysis (student's choice)

review.value: Over 80 percent of purchase decisions begin online, even for products ultimately purchased in physical stores; a brand that is invisible in search and social during the research phase is invisible during the decision phase

Targeting by search intent is typically more effective than targeting by demographics; a 55-year-old searching 'best skateboard for beginners' is a better target for a skate brand than a 22-year-old who has shown no interest. Behavior beats demographics in digital

Most brands can describe their funnel but cannot describe their customer's actual journey; mapping real journeys exposes surprising gaps, such as a 30 percent drop-off at a checkout step or a lack of content for the consideration phase
Customers expect instant, accurate answers in micro-moments; brands that load slowly, have unclear content, or require too many clicks lose the moment to competitors who are ready

learning_objectives: Explain how search engines crawl, index, and rank content using relevance and authority signals
Conduct keyword research using tools like Ahrefs, SEMrush, and Google Keyword Planner to identify high-value opportunities
Differentiate on-page SEO (content, metadata, structure), off-page SEO (backlinks, brand signals), and technical SEO (site speed, crawlability)
Audit a live website for common SEO issues and propose prioritized fixes
readings: Chaffey & Ellis-Chadwick, Digital Marketing (8th ed.), Ch. 8 - Digital Media Channels (SEO section)
Moz, 'The Beginner's Guide to SEO' (comprehensive online reference)
Google Search Central Documentation, 'Search Essentials' and 'SEO Starter Guide'
Ahrefs Blog, 'Keyword Research: The Beginner's Guide' and 'How Google Works'

lecture_notes.introduction: Search Engine Optimization remains one of the highest-ROI marketing channels because it captures customers with active intent at zero marginal cost per click. When someone searches 'best CRM for small business', they are raising their hand saying 'I am thinking about buying a CRM now.' Unlike display advertising, which interrupts unrelated activity, search captures intent. Google processes 8.5 billion searches per day (over 90,000 per second), and the top three organic results capture over 50 percent of all clicks, while position 10 captures under 3 percent. The difference between ranking first and tenth can mean millions in annual revenue. Consider HubSpot: the company built a multi-billion dollar business largely on the back of its blog and resource library, ranking first for hundreds of high-intent keywords like 'how to write an email newsletter' and 'marketing automation templates.' Each piece of content is essentially a salesperson that works 24/7, attracts prospects at the top of the funnel, and requires no additional budget after it is written. But SEO is also brutally competitive, and Google's algorithm has grown sophisticated enough to detect manipulation. The era of keyword-stuffed content, link schemes, and black-hat tactics is over. Modern SEO rewards genuinely helpful content, strong technical foundations, and authentic authority signals. This session teaches you how search actually works, how to identify opportunities where you can realistically win, and how to audit a site to find the highest-leverage fixes.
key_concepts: How search engines work: crawling (bots follow links to discover pages), indexing (storing and understanding content), ranking (ordering results by relevance and authority for each query)

Ranking factors: content relevance (does the page answer the query?), authority (PageRank from quality backlinks), user experience (Core Web Vitals, mobile-friendly), freshness (recently updated), and E-E-A-T (Experience, Expertise, Authoritativeness, Trustworthiness)

Keyword research: identifying search terms that real users type, with volume (how often searched), difficulty (how competitive), and intent (what the searcher wants); tools like Ahrefs and SEMrush provide all three metrics

Long-tail keywords: specific, lower-volume phrases (e.g., 'best running shoes for flat feet women') that together comprise 70 percent of all searches; easier to rank for and often higher-converting than head terms

On-page SEO: elements under your direct control including title tags (most important single factor), meta descriptions, headings (H1, H2, H3), URL structure, internal linking, image alt text, and body content quality and length

Technical SEO: site architecture, crawlability (robots.txt, sitemap.xml), rendering (JavaScript SEO), page speed (Core Web Vitals: LCP, INP, CLS), mobile responsiveness, HTTPS, structured data (Schema.org markup)

Off-page SEO: signals external to the site, primarily backlinks from authoritative domains; quality matters more than quantity (one link from the New York Times is worth thousands from low-quality sites)
E-A-T and YMYL: for 'Your Money or Your Life' topics (health, finance, legal), Google applies extra scrutiny to author credentials and site reputation; these niches require demonstrable expertise

Search Engine Results Page (SERP) features: featured snippets, People Also Ask boxes, knowledge panels, image packs, and local packs; these features can push organic results below the fold or provide new opportunities to capture clicks

Algorithm updates: Google releases thousands of updates per year, with a few (Core Updates, Helpful Content Update, Product Reviews Update) causing major ranking shifts; staying current is a continuous process
lecture_notes.demonstration: Live SEO audit of a mid-sized e-commerce site. Open Ahrefs Site Audit (or free tools like Screaming Frog and Google Search Console), crawl the site, and systematically review: (1) Technical health: crawl errors, broken links, missing meta tags, duplicate content, slow pages flagged by Core Web Vitals. (2) On-page opportunities: pages targeting vague keywords, missing H1s, title tags over 60 characters, thin content pages under 300 words. (3) Backlink profile: total referring domains, quality of linking sites, anchor text distribution, lost backlinks. (4) Keyword gap analysis: keywords competitors rank for that the audited site does not. Create a prioritized fix list ranked by estimated impact divided by effort. For example, fixing 47 pages with duplicate title tags might be a 2-hour fix that could improve traffic by 8 percent, while building new content for a 1000-volume keyword might require 20 hours for a smaller gain.
setup_requirements: Ahrefs free trial or SEMrush free account (both offer limited but usable free tiers)
Google Search Console verified for a test site
Screaming Frog SEO Spider (free for up to 500 URLs)
A chosen target website for audit practice

review.value: Matching content to search intent is more important than matching keywords; Google has gotten very good at understanding that 'running shoes' and 'best running shoes' have different intents and deserve different result types (product pages vs review listicles)

New or low-authority sites should target long-tail keywords (3-5 word phrases) where competition is low; ranking for 100 long-tail keywords each driving 50 visits per month produces the same traffic as ranking for one high-volume keyword and is dramatically easier

Great content on a broken site will not rank; ensure technical fundamentals (crawlability, speed, mobile-friendliness, HTTPS) before investing in content or link building. Use Google Search Console's Core Web Vitals report to prioritize technical fixes

Despite algorithm changes, high-quality backlinks from authoritative sites remain one of the strongest ranking signals; earning links through remarkable content, PR, and relationships is how modern SEO builds authority

learning_objectives: Design a content marketing strategy aligned to customer journey stages and business objectives
Apply the topic cluster model to organize content for SEO and user experience
Create an editorial calendar that balances content types, channels, and production capacity
Measure content performance using engagement, SEO, and conversion metrics
readings: Chaffey & Ellis-Chadwick, Digital Marketing (8th ed.), Ch. 9 - Marketing Communications (content section)
Joe Pulizzi, Content Inc.: How Entrepreneurs Use Content to Build Massive Audiences, Ch. 1-5
HubSpot Academy, 'Topic Cluster Model' (free course and reference)
Harvard Business Review, 'What's Your Content Marketing Strategy?' (2022)

lecture_notes.introduction: Content marketing is the practice of attracting customers by creating genuinely useful content rather than interrupting them with advertising. The idea is not new; John Deere's magazine The Furrow has been helping farmers since 1895 and still reaches 1.5 million readers. But the internet made content marketing accessible to any business with a laptop and something valuable to say. HubSpot built a multi-billion dollar company by publishing helpful marketing content that answered the questions their target customers were already asking in search engines. Red Bull built a media company that happens to sell energy drinks, producing videos and events that attract hundreds of millions of viewers. The reason content marketing works is simple: people do not want more ads, but they desperately need answers, education, entertainment, and inspiration. When a brand provides those things for free, it earns attention, trust, and eventually business. But content marketing has a failure mode: publishing lots of mediocre content that nobody reads is worse than publishing nothing, because it consumes resources without producing results. Most brands that try content marketing quit within 12 months because they expect instant traction. The brands that win are the ones that commit for 18-36 months, create content that is genuinely better than competitors, and integrate content with SEO, email, social, and sales in a coherent system. This session teaches you how to build that system: how to pick topics that matter, how to organize them into clusters that search engines reward, how to plan production capacity realistically, and how to measure whether the work is producing results.
key_concepts: Content marketing definition: creating and distributing valuable, relevant, consistent content to attract and retain a clearly defined audience, with the goal of driving profitable customer action (Content Marketing Institute definition)

Content types: blog posts, long-form guides, ebooks and whitepapers, videos (YouTube, TikTok, Instagram Reels), podcasts, webinars, infographics, case studies, email newsletters, templates and tools, interactive content (calculators, quizzes)

Content journey mapping: top-of-funnel content (educational, broad, introduces the problem), middle-of-funnel (solution-oriented, comparison, case studies), bottom-of-funnel (product-specific, free trials, demos, pricing pages)

Topic cluster model: a pillar page covers a broad topic comprehensively, linked to 20-30 cluster pages each covering a subtopic in depth; this structure improves SEO by establishing topical authority and internal linking
Editorial calendar: a planning tool showing what content will be produced, by whom, when, through which channels; balances content types, topics, and production capacity to avoid bottlenecks

Content production process: ideation (topic research), outlining (structure and key points), drafting, editing, design (images, graphics), SEO optimization, publication, promotion, measurement, iteration

Repurposing strategy: transforming one piece of content into many formats (blog post becomes video, podcast, email series, social posts, infographic); extends reach and maximizes ROI of content production

Evergreen vs timely content: evergreen content (how-to guides, fundamentals) provides long-term traffic; timely content (news commentary, trend analysis) spikes quickly but decays; a healthy mix includes both

Content distribution: creating content is only half the work; distribution through SEO, email lists, social sharing, paid amplification, and partnerships determines whether content actually reaches audiences

Content ROI measurement: vanity metrics (views, likes) vs business metrics (assisted conversions, email signups, leads generated, pipeline influenced, revenue attributed); mature content programs track the full chain from content to customer
lecture_notes.demonstration: Build a topic cluster for a B2B SaaS company selling project management software. Pillar topic: 'Project Management for Remote Teams'. Cluster topics (20): how to onboard remote employees, best collaboration tools comparison, running effective virtual standups, managing time zones in distributed teams, async vs sync communication, measuring remote team productivity, preventing remote team burnout, hiring remote developers, remote team building activities, handling difficult conversations remotely, remote team OKRs and goal setting, documentation best practices for distributed teams, and so on. For each cluster page, identify: target keyword with monthly volume and difficulty, search intent, target word count, internal link targets, and conversion call-to-action. The pillar page links to all clusters, each cluster links back to the pillar, and related clusters cross-link. After 12 months of execution, this 21-piece cluster can drive 50,000+ monthly organic visits and establish the brand as the topical authority on remote project management.
setup_requirements: Airtable or Trello for editorial calendar template
Ahrefs or SEMrush for topic research and keyword analysis
Google Docs for collaborative content drafting
Canva or Figma for content graphics and social media assets

review.value: Content marketing typically takes 6-12 months to show meaningful traction and 18-36 months to reach its full potential; brands that quit early waste their investment. Commit to a 24-month minimum before judging results

Publishing one outstanding, comprehensive 3000-word guide per month beats publishing four mediocre 800-word posts; Google's Helpful Content Update explicitly penalizes low-value content churn. Resist the temptation to publish for the sake of publishing

review.key: Topic Clusters Beat Random Blog Posts
review.value: Organizing content into topic clusters around pillar pages signals topical authority to Google and provides a better user experience. A random blog with unrelated posts rarely ranks, while a focused cluster can dominate an entire subject area

A great piece of content that nobody sees has zero value; plan distribution (email, social, paid, outreach) before you start writing. The best content marketers spend at least as much time promoting content as creating it

class_schedule.topic: Search Engine Marketing (SEM) and Paid Search
learning_objectives: Explain how auction-based bidding works in Google Ads and what determines ad rank
Structure a paid search account with campaigns, ad groups, keywords, ads, and extensions
Optimize for Quality Score by aligning keyword, ad copy, and landing page relevance
Calculate campaign economics: CPC, CTR, conversion rate, CPA, ROAS, and break-even analysis
readings: Chaffey & Ellis-Chadwick, Digital Marketing (8th ed.), Ch. 8 - Digital Media Channels (SEM section)
Perry Marshall, Ultimate Guide to Google Ads (7th ed.), Ch. 1-6
Google Ads Help Center, 'How Google Ads auctions work' and 'Quality Score'
Search Engine Land, 'The Complete Guide to Ad Rank in Google Ads' (2023)

lecture_notes.introduction: Paid search is the workhorse of digital marketing because it captures high-intent traffic at the exact moment someone is looking for a solution. When a plumbing company pays Google for the top ad on 'emergency plumber near me', they are intercepting someone with a broken pipe right now, not showing a banner ad to a person reading unrelated news. The economics can be extraordinary: a lawyer bidding on 'car accident attorney' might pay 200 dollars per click, but if one in twenty clicks becomes a case worth 30,000 dollars in fees, that is a 1,500 dollar revenue per click at a 200 dollar cost. On the other end, a hobby accessories store might pay 40 cents per click for long-tail keywords and sustain healthy margins. Google Ads alone generated 264 billion dollars in revenue in 2023, more than the GDP of most countries, because it works. But paid search is also where marketing budgets disappear fastest when executed poorly. Most small businesses who run their own Google Ads campaigns waste 20-50 percent of their budget on the wrong keywords, poorly matched searches, or broken landing pages. The difference between a profitable and unprofitable campaign comes down to discipline: rigorous keyword research, tight ad group structure, relevant ad copy, strong landing pages, meticulous negative keyword management, and constant optimization. This session gives you the mental model and the math to understand paid search economics, and the tactical knowledge to set up campaigns that actually generate profit.
key_concepts: Auction mechanics: Google holds a real-time auction for every search query; advertisers bid for placement, and the winner is determined not just by bid but by Ad Rank = Bid x Quality Score x other factors
Quality Score: Google's 1-10 rating of keyword relevance based on expected CTR, ad relevance to the search, and landing page experience; higher Quality Score means lower CPC and better ad positions

Match types: broad match (matches related searches), phrase match (includes the phrase in the query), exact match (matches only the exact term or close variants), negative match (excludes searches containing certain terms)
Account structure: Account > Campaigns (budget, goals, location, schedule) > Ad Groups (tightly themed keyword sets) > Keywords + Ads + Landing Pages; good structure is the foundation of everything
Ad extensions: site links, callouts, structured snippets, call extensions, location extensions, lead form extensions; extensions can increase CTR by 10-15 percent at no extra cost

Bidding strategies: manual CPC (full control), enhanced CPC (manual with AI adjustments), target CPA (pay what a conversion is worth), target ROAS (maintain a return ratio), maximize conversions (spend budget for most conversions)
Negative keywords: the unsung hero of paid search; adding 'free', 'cheap', 'diy', 'jobs' and irrelevant terms as negatives prevents wasted spend on searches that cannot convert
Landing page optimization: sending ad traffic to a dedicated landing page with a single clear CTA converts 3-5x better than sending it to a home page; match the landing page content to the ad message

Attribution models: last click (traditional default), first click, linear, time decay, position-based, data-driven (ML-based); each model tells a different story about which campaigns deserve credit for conversions

Campaign economics: Cost Per Click (CPC), Click-Through Rate (CTR), Conversion Rate (CVR), Cost Per Acquisition (CPA), Return on Ad Spend (ROAS = revenue / ad spend), Lifetime Value (LTV); understanding these relationships is essential for knowing whether a campaign is profitable
lecture_notes.demonstration: Build a Google Ads campaign for a hypothetical meal kit subscription service from scratch. Step 1: Keyword research in Google Keyword Planner yields terms like 'meal kit delivery' (60K searches, $8.50 avg CPC, high competition), 'healthy meal delivery service' (22K searches, $12 CPC), and 'vegetarian meal kits' (8K searches, $6 CPC, lower competition). Step 2: Campaign structure with three ad groups: brand terms, generic meal kit terms, and dietary-specific terms. Each ad group has 10-15 tightly related keywords. Step 3: Write 3 ad variations per group with headlines matching the keyword intent, descriptions highlighting unique value (30-minute meals, organic ingredients, no commitment), and site link extensions to popular recipes. Step 4: Calculate economics: at $10 CPC, 3 percent CVR, first-order value of $30, and LTV of $180, what is allowable CPA? ROAS break-even at first order alone: $10 / 0.03 = $333 CPA, but only $30 revenue, so first order loses money. With LTV of $180, break-even CPA is $180, still losing money. To be profitable, need to improve CVR to 10 percent, CPC down to $6, or LTV up to $400. Walk through negotiating these levers.
setup_requirements: Google Ads account (free to create, pay-per-click billing)
Google Keyword Planner access (requires Ads account)
Landing page tool (Unbounce, Instapage, or simple custom HTML)
Google Analytics 4 with conversion tracking set up

review.value: A higher Quality Score means paying less per click for the same position; improving Quality Score from 5 to 8 can reduce CPC by 30-50 percent, effectively doubling your budget. Invest in relevance: keyword, ad copy, and landing page must all match tightly

Review search terms reports weekly and add irrelevant queries as negative keywords; most campaigns waste 20-30 percent of budget on searches that will never convert. 'Free', 'cheap', 'jobs', and competitor brand names are common negatives
Before running a single ad, calculate break-even CPA based on conversion rate, order value, and customer lifetime value. Campaigns without clear unit economics are gambling, not marketing

Small changes to landing pages (headline, CTA, form fields, social proof) can double conversion rates; running A/B tests on landing pages is higher leverage than tweaking ad copy because conversion rate multiplies through the entire funnel

learning_objectives: Compare the major social platforms (Instagram, TikTok, LinkedIn, YouTube, X, Facebook) by audience, algorithm, and content type
Develop a social media content strategy aligned with brand voice, audience, and business objectives
Create a content calendar balancing educational, entertaining, inspirational, and promotional content
Measure organic social performance using reach, engagement, follower growth, and referral metrics
readings: Chaffey & Ellis-Chadwick, Digital Marketing (8th ed.), Ch. 9 - Marketing Communications (social section)
Gary Vaynerchuk, Jab, Jab, Jab, Right Hook: How to Tell Your Story in a Noisy Social World
Hootsuite, 'Social Media Trends 2024' annual report
Harvard Business Review, 'How to Build a Social Media Strategy That Aligns with Your Business Goals' (2023)

lecture_notes.introduction: Social media is where brands live or die with consumers under 40. TikTok alone reaches over a billion active users, and for Gen Z, TikTok has replaced Google as the primary search engine for restaurants, products, and recommendations. Instagram drives an estimated 35 percent of all e-commerce discovery. LinkedIn is the dominant channel for B2B lead generation. YouTube is the second largest search engine in the world. Ignoring social is not an option for any brand targeting modern consumers, but throwing content at social without a strategy is equally fatal. The social algorithms are punishing: most posts from business accounts reach less than 5 percent of their own followers organically. To win organically on social, brands must produce content people actually want to share, at a frequency and quality competitors cannot match, with an understanding of how each platform's algorithm works. Glossier built a billion dollar brand almost entirely through Instagram, making beauty an interactive conversation rather than a monologue, and positioning customers as the heroes of the brand story. Duolingo leveraged TikTok to turn a previously obscure language learning app into a cultural phenomenon by building an eccentric, self-aware character around their owl mascot. These successes are not accidents; they are the product of strategic thinking about platform, audience, and content that most brands never do. This session teaches you how to think about organic social strategically, how to choose the right platforms for your audience, and how to produce content that actually earns attention in a hostile algorithmic environment.
key_concepts: Platform selection: go where your audience is, not where you personally enjoy posting; LinkedIn for B2B professionals, TikTok for Gen Z and growing millennials, Instagram for broad consumer, YouTube for how-to and entertainment, X for news and B2B thought leadership

Algorithm fundamentals: most platforms reward engagement velocity (early likes, comments, shares), watch time (video), saves and shares (more valuable than likes), and consistent posting; the algorithm is not your enemy, it is a signal detector

Content pillars: 3-5 core themes the brand posts about consistently; pillars give the content strategy focus and help audiences know what to expect (e.g., Nike's pillars: athlete stories, product drops, training tips, cultural moments)

Content types: educational (how-to, tips), entertaining (humor, trending formats), inspirational (stories, quotes), promotional (products, offers); the 80/20 rule suggests 80 percent non-promotional, 20 percent promotional

Native content: content designed specifically for each platform's format rather than cross-posted from one platform to another; TikToks do not work on LinkedIn, LinkedIn carousels do not work on Instagram, adapt the content

Brand voice: a consistent personality across all social content; Wendy's sarcastic wit, Nike's empowering tone, Patagonia's activist voice; voice is what makes followers loyal even when individual posts vary

Community management: responding to comments and DMs, engaging with other accounts, building genuine relationships with followers; most brands treat social as a broadcast channel and miss the 'social' part entirely
User-Generated Content (UGC): content created by customers featuring the brand; Glossier and GoPro built empires on UGC, which is more trusted than branded content and cheaper to source
Social listening: monitoring brand mentions, competitor activity, and industry conversations; tools like Brandwatch, Sprout Social, and Meltwater reveal what customers actually think

Metrics hierarchy: vanity metrics (followers, likes) vs engagement rate (engagements / reach), vs business metrics (website clicks, conversions, assisted revenue); focus on business metrics once past early stages
lecture_notes.demonstration: Analyze Duolingo's TikTok strategy in depth. Duolingo's account has over 11 million followers and routinely gets tens of millions of views per video. Study their content: the mascot appears in irreverent, trending TikTok formats, often commenting on pop culture, dancing, or being chaotic in the office. Content pillars: (1) reacting to trends, (2) Duo the owl being menacing about not practicing, (3) behind-the-scenes office culture, (4) education content sneaked in. Analyze the algorithm signals they earn: high comment volume (people respond to the mascot as if he is a real character), high share rate (content is absurd enough to want to show friends), high save rate on educational content, and strong watch time (videos are under 30 seconds). Then compare to a typical brand TikTok that posts product shots and corporate messages and gets 200 views. The lesson: native formats, strong character, and embracing platform culture beat polished corporate content on organic social.
setup_requirements: Buffer, Later, or Hootsuite free trial for social scheduling
Canva Pro for content creation (free tier sufficient for basic work)
Platform accounts on Instagram, TikTok, LinkedIn, YouTube for practice
Screen recording and video editing tool (CapCut is free and powerful)

review.value: Most brands spread themselves too thin across platforms and produce mediocre content everywhere; better to dominate two platforms than be invisible on six. Start by choosing the one or two platforms where your customers actually spend time

Cross-posting the same content to every platform is the mark of amateur social marketing; each platform has its own format, culture, and algorithm, and content must be tailored. A TikTok that works natively will not work as an Instagram carousel

100,000 engaged followers are worth more than 1 million passive followers; engagement rate (likes + comments + shares divided by reach) is the truer measure of social health. Brands that buy followers sabotage their algorithm standing

Organic social success compounds: one viral video brings new followers who engage with subsequent content, which signals the algorithm to show content more broadly. This flywheel takes 6-12 months to start but accelerates thereafter

learning_objectives: Explain the fundamental differences between paid social, paid search, and display advertising in terms of intent and targeting
Design campaigns in Meta Ads Manager using campaign objectives, audience targeting, placements, and creative formats
Apply audience targeting strategies including custom audiences, lookalike audiences, and interest targeting
Measure paid social performance using platform metrics and attribution to web and app conversions
readings: Chaffey & Ellis-Chadwick, Digital Marketing (8th ed.), Ch. 8 - Digital Media Channels (paid social section)
Meta for Business, 'Advertiser Playbook' (official documentation)
Jon Loomer Digital Blog, 'Facebook Ads Strategy Guide' (practical reference)
Harvard Business Review, 'The Death of the Targeting Cookie' (2022)

lecture_notes.introduction: Paid social advertising reaches audiences with a fundamentally different proposition from paid search: instead of capturing existing intent, paid social creates interest by interrupting users with relevant content in a context they enjoy. Meta (Facebook and Instagram) generated 134 billion dollars in ad revenue in 2023, TikTok surpassed 20 billion, and LinkedIn exceeded 15 billion in B2B advertising alone. These platforms can drive staggering business results when executed well: Allbirds scaled from zero to 100 million dollars in sales primarily through Facebook and Instagram ads, and thousands of D2C brands have built entire businesses on paid social. But paid social also burns through budget faster than any other channel when mismanaged, because audiences are not actively searching for solutions; they are passively scrolling, and the brand must earn every second of attention with creative, relevance, and value. The game changed in 2021 when Apple's iOS 14.5 introduced App Tracking Transparency, shattering the precise targeting and attribution that made paid social so effective. Meta lost over 10 billion dollars in revenue, and the entire industry had to rebuild on new infrastructure: more reliance on first-party data, more creative-driven targeting, and probabilistic attribution instead of deterministic. This session teaches you the mental model for paid social, the mechanics of campaign creation, and the strategic approaches that work in the post-iOS 14 world where targeting is harder but brands with strong creative and measurement systems still thrive.
key_concepts: Campaign objectives: Meta's campaign structure starts with an objective (awareness, traffic, engagement, leads, sales, app installs); the objective tells the algorithm what to optimize for and dramatically changes delivery

Audience targeting methods: demographics (age, gender, location), interests (declared or inferred from behavior), custom audiences (your customer list uploaded), website custom audiences (retargeting site visitors), lookalike audiences (new users similar to your customers)

Placements: Facebook Feed, Instagram Feed, Stories, Reels, Messenger, Audience Network; placements differ dramatically in performance, and 'automatic placements' usually works better than manual selection

Creative formats: single image, carousel, video, collection, instant experience; video ads typically outperform static images, but vertical video for Reels and Stories is required to avoid letterboxing

Ad copy structure: hook in the first 3 seconds (for video) or first line (for text), problem or promise, solution or proof, call to action; scroll-stopping creative is more valuable than precise targeting in 2024

Bidding strategies: lowest cost (default, maximize results within budget), bid cap (control maximum bid), cost cap (average cost target), ROAS target (maintain return ratio); Meta's machine learning generally performs best with fewer constraints
Creative testing: systematically test creative variations (hooks, visuals, CTAs, offers) while holding audience and bidding constant; creative is the biggest lever for performance in paid social

Attribution challenges: after iOS 14.5, deterministic tracking of conversions is limited; brands use Conversions API (server-side tracking), first-party data, and incrementality testing to measure real impact
Retargeting: showing ads to users who already visited your website or engaged with your content; retargeting typically has 3-5x higher conversion rates than cold audiences but is limited in reach
Frequency caps: showing the same ad too often (over 3-5 impressions per week) causes ad fatigue, where CTR drops and CPM rises; rotating creative and managing frequency preserves performance
lecture_notes.demonstration: Build a Meta Ads campaign for a subscription coffee brand targeting specialty coffee enthusiasts. Campaign objective: Conversions (the algorithm will optimize for purchases). Audiences: (1) Custom audience of existing customers (excluded from acquisition campaigns). (2) Lookalike of top 5 percent LTV customers (1 percent similarity for precision). (3) Interest audience targeting people who follow specialty coffee brands (Blue Bottle, Stumptown, Intelligentsia) aged 25-45 in urban US. (4) Retargeting audience of website visitors in last 30 days who did not purchase. Creatives: video ad showing the coffee brewing process in 15 seconds with a first-purchase discount offer, plus carousel showing 5 subscription tiers. Budget: $100/day split across audiences. Predicted performance based on industry benchmarks: $25 CPM, 1.5 percent CTR to website, 3 percent site conversion rate to purchase. Walk through: $100 / $25 CPM = 4000 impressions, 4000 * 0.015 = 60 clicks, 60 * 0.03 = 1.8 conversions per day. At $30 AOV that is $54 revenue per $100 spend, losing money on first purchase. Strategy: the campaign is only profitable accounting for 3-month LTV of $90, showing why LTV understanding is critical.
setup_requirements: Meta Business Suite account (free)
Meta Pixel installed on a test website for tracking
Meta Ads Manager for campaign creation (requires payment method)
Canva or Figma for ad creative design

review.value: After iOS 14 limited targeting precision, creative quality became the dominant performance driver; Meta's algorithm can find the right audience if the creative is good enough to engage them. Invest heavily in creative testing, not audience fiddling

Retargeting website visitors who did not convert typically generates 3-5x higher conversion rates than cold audiences; every brand should run retargeting campaigns as a baseline. Exclude recent converters to avoid wasting budget

review.key: Objective Matters More Than You Think
review.value: Selecting the wrong campaign objective tanks performance; a 'traffic' campaign gets clicks from people unlikely to convert, while a 'conversions' campaign gets fewer clicks from people more likely to buy. Always optimize for your true business goal

Reported ROAS in ad platforms overstates actual impact because it counts conversions from people who would have bought anyway; incrementality testing (holdout groups) reveals true lift and is the gold standard for measurement

learning_objectives: Design an email marketing strategy that balances broadcast campaigns, automated flows, and transactional messages
Build segmented email lists using behavioral, demographic, and lifecycle data
Configure marketing automation workflows in platforms like HubSpot, Klaviyo, or Mailchimp
Measure and optimize email performance using deliverability, engagement, and conversion metrics
readings: Chaffey & Ellis-Chadwick, Digital Marketing (8th ed.), Ch. 9 - Marketing Communications (email section)
Chad White, Email Marketing Rules: Checklists, Frameworks, and 150 Best Practices (4th ed.)
Klaviyo, 'Email Marketing Benchmarks Report' (annual industry report)
Harvard Business Review, 'The Promise of Marketing Automation' (2022)

lecture_notes.introduction: Email marketing remains the most profitable channel in digital marketing by ROI, despite being one of the oldest. Industry research from the Data and Marketing Association consistently shows email returning 36-42 dollars per dollar spent, higher than any other channel by a wide margin. The reason is simple: email is owned media. Unlike social followers (whose access you rent from Meta or TikTok) or search visibility (controlled by Google), your email list is an asset you own and can reach any time without paying a middleman. D2C brands like Warby Parker, Bonobos, and Away drive 20-30 percent of their revenue from email, and for mature e-commerce businesses, automated email flows alone can drive 40 percent of revenue with zero ongoing human effort after setup. But email marketing also has trust as its currency, and that trust is fragile. Sending irrelevant, too-frequent, or manipulative emails causes unsubscribes, spam complaints, and deliverability damage that compounds over time. Inbox providers like Gmail and Apple Mail increasingly use machine learning to filter promotional email away from users, and poorly performing senders get buried in Promotions tabs or worse. This session teaches you how to build email programs that customers actually want to receive: segmented properly, personalized meaningfully, automated thoughtfully, and measured rigorously. You will learn the difference between broadcast campaigns and automated flows, why flows typically drive 2-3x the revenue of campaigns at a fraction of the effort, and how to construct a complete email automation system for a modern business.
key_concepts: Email types: broadcast campaigns (one-time sends to segments, like a weekly newsletter or promotional email), automated flows (triggered by behavior, run forever), transactional (order confirmations, password resets, triggered by specific actions)

List building: growing the email list through website signup forms, exit-intent popups, lead magnets (ebooks, discounts, templates), checkout options, social media, and partnerships; quality of signup source predicts long-term engagement

Segmentation: dividing the list into groups based on behavior (purchased in last 30 days), demographics (location), engagement (clicks in last 60 days), or lifecycle stage (new, active, at-risk, churned); segmentation dramatically improves engagement and deliverability

Welcome series: an automated sequence sent to new subscribers, typically 3-7 emails introducing the brand, setting expectations, offering value; welcome emails get 4x higher open rates than regular campaigns and drive 320 percent more revenue

Abandoned cart flow: automated emails sent to customers who added items to cart but did not complete checkout, typically sent 1-24 hours later; recovers 10-25 percent of abandoned carts with strong creative and urgency
Post-purchase flow: order confirmation, shipping updates, review request, cross-sell recommendations, replenishment reminders; extends customer LTV and encourages repeat purchases
Win-back flow: targeted at subscribers who have not engaged in 60-90 days with a special offer or reason to return; saves customers before they churn completely

Deliverability fundamentals: sender reputation (measured by engagement rates), authentication (SPF, DKIM, DMARC records), list hygiene (removing unengaged subscribers), and content quality (avoiding spam triggers like all caps, excessive links, spammy phrases)

Personalization vs segmentation: personalization inserts individual data (first name, product recommendations based on browse history); segmentation sends different content to groups; both work, and combined they multiply effectiveness

Key metrics: open rate (proxy for subject line and sender reputation), click rate (measures content relevance), conversion rate (clicks to purchase), revenue per email, list growth rate, unsubscribe rate, spam complaint rate
lecture_notes.demonstration: Design an abandoned cart flow for a skincare e-commerce brand using Klaviyo. Trigger: customer added product to cart but did not complete checkout within 1 hour. Flow structure: Email 1 sent 1 hour after abandonment, subject line 'You left something behind', content showing the abandoned product, reviews, and a direct checkout link. Email 2 sent 24 hours later if not purchased, subject line 'Still thinking it over?', content emphasizing the product benefits and social proof with customer testimonials. Email 3 sent 72 hours later if not purchased, subject line 'Here is 10 percent off to help you decide', offering a limited-time discount code valid for 48 hours. Email 4 sent 7 days later if still not purchased, subject line 'Last chance on these items', final reminder before removing from flow. Predicted performance: email 1 recovers 5-8 percent of abandoners, email 2 recovers 3-5 percent, email 3 recovers 2-4 percent, for total 10-17 percent recovery rate. At 60 dollar average cart value, for every 1000 abandoners, this flow recovers 6000-10,200 dollars in revenue automatically. Calculate ROI: if the brand has 500 abandoned carts per week, the flow drives 300K+ in annual revenue with zero incremental labor after setup.
setup_requirements: Klaviyo, Mailchimp, or HubSpot free trial account
Test e-commerce store (Shopify free trial) for flow testing
Email design template (drag-and-drop or HTML)
List of test email addresses for flow verification

review.value: Automated email flows (welcome, abandoned cart, post-purchase, win-back) typically drive 40-60 percent of email revenue from 10 percent of the effort; mature email programs prioritize flows over endless campaign production

Sending the same email to everyone on your list is lazy and counterproductive; segmenting by engagement, purchase history, and lifecycle stage can 2-3x open and click rates while reducing unsubscribes. Start with at least three segments: new, active, at-risk

Inbox providers reward senders whose recipients engage (opens, clicks, replies) and punish those with high complaint rates. Regularly remove unengaged subscribers (after win-back attempts), even though it shrinks the list, because the alternative is being filtered to spam

Every dollar invested in growing and nurturing an email list compounds over years; unlike ad spend that vanishes when you stop, an engaged email list continues producing revenue for years. Prioritize list building as a long-term asset strategy

learning_objectives: Differentiate the tiers of influencers (nano, micro, macro, mega) and their trade-offs in reach, cost, and engagement
Develop a creator partnership strategy aligned with brand goals, budget, and target audience
Structure influencer contracts covering deliverables, usage rights, FTC disclosure, and performance metrics
Measure influencer campaign impact using attribution, engagement, and incrementality
readings: Chaffey & Ellis-Chadwick, Digital Marketing (8th ed.), Ch. 9 - Influencer marketing section
Neal Schaffer, The Age of Influence: The Power of Influencers to Elevate Your Brand, Ch. 1-5
FTC, 'Disclosures 101 for Social Media Influencers' (official guidance)
Influencer Marketing Hub, 'The State of Influencer Marketing 2024' annual report

lecture_notes.introduction: Influencer marketing has grown from a curiosity into a 24 billion dollar global industry, and for many D2C brands it is now the single largest marketing channel. The logic is simple: consumers trust other people more than they trust brands, and in a saturated advertising environment, recommendations from trusted voices cut through in ways banner ads cannot. When Gymshark partnered with fitness influencers on Instagram and YouTube in the early 2010s, they built a billion dollar activewear brand from nothing, bypassing traditional retail entirely. When Glossier used beauty influencers and regular customers as advocates, they turned Instagram into a discovery engine for their products. But influencer marketing is also the Wild West of modern marketing, with rampant fraud (fake followers, fake engagement), unclear ROI measurement, FTC compliance issues, and creative conflicts between brand control and creator authenticity. A single poorly-chosen influencer can waste six figures on sponsored posts that reach almost nobody, or worse, generate backlash when an ill-fit creator's audience rejects the partnership. This session teaches you to think about influencer marketing like any other paid channel: with discipline about audience fit, transparent contracts, clear performance metrics, and rigorous attribution. You will learn how to vet influencers beyond vanity metrics, how to structure partnerships that protect both parties, and how to measure whether the campaign actually moved the business.
key_concepts: Influencer tiers: nano (1K-10K followers, highest engagement, lowest cost, most authentic), micro (10K-100K, sweet spot for most brands, strong niche expertise), macro (100K-1M, broader reach, higher cost), mega (1M+, celebrity tier, lowest engagement rates but massive awareness)

Engagement rate as quality signal: engagement rate (likes plus comments divided by followers) is the primary quality metric; nano influencers typically have 5-10 percent engagement while mega influencers often have under 1 percent

Audience quality vetting: tools like Modash, HypeAuditor, and Social Blade reveal fake followers, audience demographics, geographic distribution, historic growth patterns (sudden spikes indicate purchased followers)

Partnership models: sponsored posts (flat fee for a post), affiliate (commission on attributed sales), gifting (free product in exchange for organic post), brand ambassador (ongoing relationship), whitelisting (brand runs ads through influencer's handle), creator licensing (brand uses creator content in its own paid ads)

Contract essentials: deliverables (platforms, formats, posting schedule), exclusivity (can influencer promote competitors?), usage rights (can brand repurpose content in ads?), approval process, payment terms, FTC disclosure requirements, termination clauses

FTC disclosure: influencers must clearly disclose material connections (paid partnership, free product, affiliate relationship); hashtags like #ad or #sponsored must be visible, not buried; lack of disclosure can result in fines and brand reputation damage

Creative brief vs creative control: effective partnerships give the creator a brief (goals, key messages, do's and don'ts) but let them produce content in their native voice; overly scripted content underperforms because audiences detect inauthenticity instantly

Attribution methods: promo codes (each influencer gets a unique code), affiliate links (tracked URLs), landing pages (dedicated page for the campaign), lift studies (measured sales lift in targeted geography), branded search (Google Trends for brand searches after campaign)
Creator economy platforms: Instagram Creator Marketplace, TikTok Creator Marketplace, YouTube BrandConnect, Grin, CreatorIQ, Aspire; these platforms help find, contract, and manage creators at scale

UGC and creator content in paid ads: the trend of repurposing influencer content as paid social creative often outperforms traditional polished ads because it feels more authentic; 'UGC ads' generate 4x higher CTR in many A/B tests
lecture_notes.demonstration: Build an influencer campaign for a new sustainable fashion brand launching a capsule collection. Target audience: women 25-35 interested in sustainability and ethical fashion. Strategy: (1) Identify 30 micro-influencers (10K-100K followers) in the sustainable fashion space using HypeAuditor, filtering for authentic engagement over 4 percent and US audience over 60 percent. (2) Gifting tier: send product to 20 influencers for organic posts (no payment, no guaranteed post). Expected: 60-70 percent will post within 30 days. (3) Paid tier: contract 10 influencers for one paid post each, 500-2000 dollars depending on reach, with clear deliverables (1 Instagram feed post, 3 Stories, 1 Reel), usage rights for 90 days paid amplification, and a unique promo code. (4) Measurement: track promo code redemptions (direct attribution), branded search lift, follower growth during campaign window, and compare to pre-campaign baseline. (5) Budget: 15,000 total (10K paid posts, 3K product, 2K amplification budget for top-performing content). Expected outcomes: 800K-1.2M total impressions, 300-500 promo code redemptions at 100 AOV = 30K-50K direct revenue, plus brand awareness lift.
setup_requirements: Modash or HypeAuditor for influencer vetting (free trial)
Instagram Creator Marketplace for outreach
Contract template (sample influencer agreement)
Promo code generation in e-commerce platform for tracking

review.value: An influencer with 20K engaged followers typically outperforms one with 500K passive followers; evaluate partnerships on engagement rate and audience quality, not raw reach. Fake followers are rampant; always vet with third-party tools

Influencers have audiences because they are trusted voices; forcing scripted corporate messaging destroys the trust and the campaign. Provide a brief with key messages, then step back and let the creator do their job

Every sponsored post must clearly disclose the material connection; the FTC has ramped up enforcement, and brands bear responsibility for influencer compliance. Build disclosure requirements into every contract and monitor actual posts

Unique promo codes, dedicated landing pages, and affiliate links are essential for attribution; campaigns without these produce 'awareness' metrics that cannot be tied to revenue. Build measurement infrastructure before launching campaigns

class_schedule.topic: Conversion Rate Optimization and A/B Testing
learning_objectives: Define conversion rate optimization (CRO) and its role in the marketing funnel
Design A/B tests with proper hypothesis formulation, sample size calculation, and statistical analysis
Identify common CRO opportunities using heuristic analysis, analytics data, and user research
Interpret A/B test results with statistical significance, confidence intervals, and business impact
readings: Chaffey & Ellis-Chadwick, Digital Marketing (8th ed.), Ch. 7 - Digital Marketing Planning
Peep Laja, Conversion Optimization: The Art and Science of Converting Visitors into Customers
Optimizely, 'The A/B Testing Handbook' (free ebook)
Evan Miller, 'How Not To Run An A/B Test' (foundational blog post)

lecture_notes.introduction: Conversion Rate Optimization is the practice of improving the percentage of website visitors who take a desired action, and it is one of the highest-leverage activities in digital marketing. The reason is compounding: if you spend 10,000 dollars on ads driving 10,000 visitors with a 2 percent conversion rate, you get 200 customers at 50 dollar CPA. Double the conversion rate to 4 percent with better landing pages, and the same 10,000 dollars produces 400 customers at 25 dollar CPA. Every other metric in your marketing funnel improves when conversion rate goes up: ROAS doubles, LTV:CAC improves, paid channels become profitable at higher CPCs, and SEO traffic generates more revenue per visit. The methodology of CRO is the scientific method applied to websites: form a hypothesis based on data and user research, design an experiment to test it, run the experiment until you reach statistical significance, then implement the winner or learn from the loser. Done rigorously, CRO produces compounding gains: a dozen successful tests per year each lifting conversion rate by 5 percent compound to over 80 percent improvement. Done poorly, CRO becomes confirmation bias theater: running underpowered tests, calling winners based on daily fluctuations, and cherry-picking results that support preconceptions. Most organizations that claim to do A/B testing are actually doing the second. This session teaches you to do it right: the statistical foundations, the experimental design principles, the analytical discipline, and the research methods that identify which tests to run in the first place.
key_concepts: Conversion rate definition: the percentage of visitors who complete a desired action (purchase, signup, form fill, add to cart); measured per page, per channel, or per segment

Macro vs micro conversions: macro conversions are primary business outcomes (purchase, subscription); micro conversions are smaller steps (add to cart, email signup, video view) that indicate progress toward the macro conversion
A/B testing methodology: split traffic between control (current version) and variant (new version), measure conversion rate for each, determine whether the difference is statistically significant
Hypothesis formulation: 'If we change X because of insight from data, then metric Y will improve because of reasoning'; a good hypothesis is falsifiable and grounded in user research or analytics

Sample size calculation: depends on baseline conversion rate, minimum detectable effect (MDE), statistical significance level (typically 95 percent), and statistical power (typically 80 percent); most teams run tests far below the required sample size

Statistical significance vs practical significance: a test can be statistically significant (unlikely due to chance) but practically insignificant (a 0.2 percent lift on a metric that does not drive revenue); focus on practically meaningful effects

Common testing mistakes: peeking at results before reaching sample size, running multiple tests on the same page simultaneously without isolation, not accounting for weekly cycles, calling winners based on directional data

Testing hierarchy: high-impact areas first (checkout page, pricing page, primary CTA buttons), then secondary areas (category pages, PDPs), then tertiary (blog, footer); 80 percent of impact comes from 20 percent of pages
Test ideation sources: user session recordings (Hotjar, FullStory), heatmaps, exit surveys, customer support tickets, competitor analysis, analytics funnel drop-off analysis, user testing recordings

Segmentation of results: a test can show no overall effect but dramatic segment effects (positive for mobile, negative for desktop); always analyze results by key segments to catch heterogeneous treatment effects
lecture_notes.demonstration: Run a sample size calculation and hypothesis for a checkout page test. Current state: checkout page with 15 form fields and 8 percent completion rate. Hypothesis: 'If we reduce the form to 6 fields (removing phone, title, company, etc.) because our analytics show 40 percent drop-off at form start, then checkout completion rate will improve by at least 15 percent relative because fewer fields reduce friction.' Sample size calculation using Optimizely or AB Test Guide calculator: baseline 8 percent, MDE 15 percent relative = target 9.2 percent, significance 95 percent, power 80 percent = required sample size approximately 15,000 visitors per variant, so 30,000 total. At current traffic of 5000 checkout starts per week, the test needs 6 weeks to reach significance. Plan: run for full 6 weeks regardless of interim results, segment results by device and traffic source, implement winner only if lift is both statistically and practically significant (minimum 10K per month revenue impact).
setup_requirements: Google Optimize successor (VWO, Optimizely, AB Tasty, or open-source GrowthBook)
Hotjar or Microsoft Clarity for session recording and heatmaps
Sample size calculator (abtestguide.com or Optimizely's)
Analytics platform with conversion tracking (GA4)

review.value: A test that reaches statistical significance with 200 visitors per variant is almost certainly a false positive; most meaningful changes require thousands of conversions to detect reliably. Calculate sample size BEFORE the test, not after

Checking results daily and stopping when the test looks favorable dramatically increases false positive rate; commit to a sample size and duration before starting, and do not stop until both are reached. Use sequential testing methods if you need to peek

Tests based on guesses or best practices usually fail (CXL research suggests 70 percent of tests produce no lift); tests based on user research (data, recordings, interviews) have much higher win rates. Spend 80 percent of CRO time on research, 20 percent on testing

Individual test wins rarely exceed 10-15 percent lift, but 20 successful tests per year compound to a 100-200 percent overall improvement; the discipline of continuous testing beats the search for home-run experiments. Make CRO a systematic program, not a project

learning_objectives: Configure Google Analytics 4 (GA4) for an e-commerce site with events, conversions, and audiences
Compare attribution models (last click, first click, linear, data-driven) and their impact on channel valuation
Interpret standard GA4 reports: Acquisition, Engagement, Monetization, and Retention
Build custom analytics dashboards that surface actionable insights, not vanity metrics
readings: Chaffey & Ellis-Chadwick, Digital Marketing (8th ed.), Ch. 10 - Evaluation and Improvement
Google Analytics Help, 'GA4 Implementation Guide' and 'Attribution Modeling'
Avinash Kaushik, Web Analytics 2.0: The Art of Online Accountability, Ch. 1-4
Simo Ahava Blog, 'GA4 Advanced Topics' (technical reference)

lecture_notes.introduction: You cannot manage what you cannot measure, and in digital marketing analytics is the nervous system that lets marketers understand what is working. Yet most marketers use analytics as a reporting tool rather than a decision tool: they generate dashboards nobody reads, celebrate vanity metrics like pageviews, and miss the insights that would actually improve business performance. Google Analytics 4 replaced Universal Analytics in July 2023 and fundamentally changed how web analytics works, moving from session-based to event-based data models. This change broke most marketers' existing reports and dashboards, forcing an industry-wide relearning. Beneath the migration pain, GA4 is a more modern and powerful tool: it uses machine learning for attribution, tracks cross-device journeys, integrates natively with BigQuery for advanced analysis, and privacy-handles the post-cookie world more gracefully. But it is also harder to use, with less intuitive reports and more configuration required. Beyond GA4, the attribution landscape in 2024 is messy: iOS 14, third-party cookie deprecation, GDPR consent requirements, and the rise of first-party data infrastructure have made the idea of a single source of truth almost extinct. Modern marketing teams use multiple data sources (GA4, platform reports, CDPs, data warehouses) and multiple attribution approaches (last click for simplicity, data-driven for strategic decisions, MMM for channel-level budget allocation). This session teaches you to think about measurement strategically, set up GA4 correctly, interpret attribution carefully, and build reporting that drives decisions instead of filling folders.
key_concepts: GA4 data model: event-based tracking where every user interaction (pageview, click, scroll, purchase) is an event with parameters; fundamentally different from Universal Analytics sessions-based model

Events and conversions: any measurable action is an event; events marked as 'conversions' count toward campaign goals and appear in acquisition reports; GA4 automatically tracks many events (scrolls, outbound clicks, video engagement)

E-commerce tracking: specific events (view_item, add_to_cart, begin_checkout, purchase) with standardized parameters enable the Monetization reports that show revenue by channel, product, and campaign
Audiences and segments: groups of users defined by behavior or attributes; can be used for analysis, remarketing to Google Ads, or as conversion goals

Attribution models: last click (last touch gets 100 percent credit), first click (first touch gets credit), linear (equal credit across touches), time decay (recent touches weighted higher), position-based (first and last touches weighted), data-driven (ML assigns credit based on actual lift)

Attribution implications: last click attribution undervalues awareness channels (display, social); data-driven attribution gives fair credit to assist touches; switching models can reallocate 30-50 percent of apparent channel value
Cross-device measurement: GA4 uses User ID (logged in users) and Google Signals (logged in to Google) to stitch cross-device journeys; critical because journeys increasingly span mobile and desktop
BigQuery integration: GA4 can export raw event data to BigQuery for SQL analysis, joining with CRM data, warehouse data, and building custom attribution models; free tier covers most small businesses

Custom dimensions and metrics: extending GA4 beyond defaults to track business-specific data (membership tier, product category, user lifetime value stage); custom dimensions enable meaningful segmentation
Privacy and consent mode: GA4 Consent Mode adjusts data collection based on user consent; for sites with GDPR visitors, consent mode and server-side tracking are essential for accurate measurement
lecture_notes.demonstration: Walk through a complete GA4 setup for a Shopify store. Step 1: Create GA4 property and install tag via Google Tag Manager. Step 2: Install the Shopify GA4 integration for automatic e-commerce event tracking (purchase, add_to_cart, begin_checkout events). Step 3: Configure conversions (mark purchase as conversion, optionally mark newsletter signup and account creation). Step 4: Set up custom dimensions for customer lifetime value tier, product category, and loyalty program status. Step 5: Build audiences for retargeting: 'cart abandoners', 'high value customers', 'product viewers last 7 days'. Step 6: Connect to Google Ads for audience sharing and Google Signals for cross-device. Step 7: Build a custom dashboard showing: revenue by channel with last-click and data-driven attribution side by side, top landing pages by conversion rate, top products by revenue, and funnel analysis from landing to purchase. Show how this dashboard would reveal that paid social appears to drive 20 percent of revenue under last click but 35 percent under data-driven, prompting a budget reallocation decision.
setup_requirements: Google Analytics 4 account (free)
Google Tag Manager account for tag deployment
Test e-commerce site with GA4 integration
Looker Studio account for dashboard building (free)

review.value: Different attribution models assign very different value to the same channel; last click undervalues awareness channels by up to 50 percent, while first click overvalues them. Use data-driven attribution for budget decisions and understand the biases of whatever model you use

Modern web analytics is event-based, not page-based; focus on meaningful user actions (add to cart, form start, video 50 percent watched) rather than generic pageviews. Events reveal engagement that pageviews hide

Pageviews, impressions, and follower counts are vanity metrics that feel good but rarely drive decisions; focus reporting on metrics that connect to revenue (conversions, CPA, ROAS, LTV) or that diagnose funnel problems (drop-off rates, time to convert)

A dashboard that nobody uses is worthless; every dashboard should answer specific business questions and prompt specific decisions. Prune dashboards aggressively and focus on the 3-5 charts that actually change behavior

learning_objectives: Identify the unique characteristics of mobile marketing including app install, push notifications, location, and mobile commerce
Design an app user acquisition strategy using paid channels and app store optimization (ASO)
Build engagement and retention loops for mobile apps using push notifications and in-app messaging
Measure mobile app KPIs including CPI, retention curves, DAU/MAU, and LTV
readings: Chaffey & Ellis-Chadwick, Digital Marketing (8th ed.), Ch. 7 - Mobile marketing section
Peter Fader, Customer Centricity: Focus on the Right Customers for Strategic Advantage
data.ai (formerly App Annie), 'State of Mobile 2024' annual report
Adjust Blog, 'Mobile Marketing Glossary' and 'App Marketing Benchmarks'

lecture_notes.introduction: Mobile is where marketing happens in 2024. Over 60 percent of global web traffic comes from mobile devices, consumers spend an average of 4.8 hours per day on their phones, and for many product categories mobile commerce has overtaken desktop. For apps, the economics are different: users spend 90 percent of mobile time in apps rather than browsers, and app users are typically 3-5x more valuable than website users because they engage more frequently and convert at higher rates. This is why brands like Starbucks built one of the most successful mobile apps in the world, with 32 million active members generating nearly a quarter of all US transactions for the company; why Uber, DoorDash, and Airbnb are essentially app-first businesses; and why Nike's SNKRS app generated over a billion dollars in sales as a standalone product. But mobile marketing is also harder than web marketing. Apps require users to install, which is a much higher friction point than visiting a website. Retention decays brutally: the average app loses 77 percent of users in the first three days after install and 90 percent within 30 days. Attribution is complicated by platform restrictions (Apple's SKAdNetwork replacing IDFA, Android's Privacy Sandbox in progress). The App Store and Play Store are winner-take-all marketplaces where the top apps dominate discovery and lesser apps are invisible. This session teaches you how to approach mobile marketing strategically: when to invest in an app versus a mobile-optimized website, how to drive installs profitably, how to design engagement loops that keep users coming back, and how to measure success in a post-IDFA world.
key_concepts: Mobile-first design: designing experiences for mobile screens first and scaling up to desktop, rather than the opposite; Google has used mobile-first indexing since 2018, penalizing sites that work poorly on mobile

App vs mobile web trade-offs: apps offer better performance, push notifications, offline support, and integration with device features; mobile web offers zero install friction and better discoverability via search; mature businesses often use both

App Store Optimization (ASO): optimizing app store listings to improve discovery and conversion; key elements are title (keyword-optimized but readable), subtitle, keywords, screenshots, preview video, ratings, and reviews

User acquisition channels for apps: paid social (Meta, TikTok, Snap), Apple Search Ads, Google App Campaigns, incentivized installs (pay users to install), influencers, and referrals; most apps use a mix depending on LTV economics
