Cost Per Install (CPI): the primary acquisition metric for apps; varies wildly from 50 cents for casual games to 50 dollars plus for fintech and enterprise apps; must be evaluated against LTV for profitability

Attribution in post-IDFA world: Apple's SKAdNetwork provides privacy-preserving attribution with aggregated data and delays; Mobile Measurement Partners (MMPs) like Adjust, AppsFlyer, and Branch provide attribution infrastructure
Retention curves: plotting the percentage of users still active on day 1, day 7, day 30, day 90; the shape of the curve reveals product-market fit (flat retention) vs churn problems (steep decay)

Engagement metrics: Daily Active Users (DAU), Monthly Active Users (MAU), DAU/MAU ratio (stickiness), session length, sessions per day, time in app; Facebook reportedly considers DAU/MAU above 50 percent indicative of product-market fit

Push notifications: the most powerful retention tool for apps, but easy to abuse; best practices include personalization, segmentation, frequency caps, opt-in permission earned through value, and clear value propositions
In-app messaging and feature discovery: onboarding flows, feature tours, contextual tooltips, and behavioral triggers help users discover features and form habits that drive retention
lecture_notes.demonstration: Analyze the user acquisition and retention strategy for Duolingo. Acquisition: Duolingo uses TikTok and Instagram organic for awareness (the Duo mascot character), paid social ads targeting language learning interests, partnerships with influencers, and strong App Store Optimization around terms like 'learn Spanish', 'learn French'. Onboarding: immediately on first launch, the app asks users to set a goal, choose a language, and start a lesson within 90 seconds, creating quick wins. Retention loops: streaks (daily practice incentive), notifications (famously aggressive reminders with the Duo character guilt-tripping users), leaderboards (social competition), gamification (XP, levels, gems). Measurement: Duolingo optimizes DAU, daily streak holders, and D1/D7/D30 retention. Calculate the economics: if Duolingo pays 3 dollars per install on paid channels, 40 percent of users retain to day 7, 15 percent retain to day 30, 5 percent convert to paid Duolingo Plus at 84 dollars per year, and average retained subscriber lifetime is 24 months, LTV is roughly 0.05 x 168 = 8.40 per install. That gives a 3 dollar CPI to 8.40 dollar LTV ratio that is profitable after product costs and platform fees. Compare to a hypothetical language app charging 1.99 per month at 2 percent conversion that would need CPI below 60 cents to break even.
setup_requirements: Free tier of Adjust, AppsFlyer, or Firebase for mobile attribution
App Store Connect access for ASO analysis of any app
Sensor Tower or data.ai free tier for market intelligence
Push notification testing tool (OneSignal free tier)

review.value: Building an app is expensive and only worthwhile if the business case justifies it; apps pay off for frequent use cases with high engagement (social, games, services with repeat usage), but fail for low-frequency use cases where mobile web is sufficient

Install count is a vanity metric without retention; a million users who all churn in 30 days is worthless, while 100,000 users who retain for years is a durable business. Focus on retention curves from day one, not installs

App Store Optimization is the mobile equivalent of SEO: free ongoing traffic that compounds as ratings improve and keyword rankings rise; neglecting ASO leaves growth on the table. Audit and iterate on store listings quarterly

Push is the most powerful and most abused mobile channel; earn permission through demonstrated value, segment aggressively, cap frequency, and measure opt-out rates. Brands that abuse push see permission rates drop to single digits

class_schedule.topic: Marketing Technology Stack and Data Infrastructure
learning_objectives: Explain the major components of a modern marketing technology stack and how they integrate
Differentiate Customer Data Platforms (CDP), Customer Relationship Management (CRM), and Data Management Platforms (DMP)
Design a martech stack for a mid-sized e-commerce business considering cost, complexity, and integration
Identify the challenges of data quality, identity resolution, and first-party data strategy
readings: Chaffey & Ellis-Chadwick, Digital Marketing (8th ed.), Ch. 3 - Digital Marketing Strategy
Scott Brinker, 'Marketing Technology Landscape' (annual report tracking 10,000+ martech vendors)
Segment, 'The Definitive Guide to Customer Data Platforms' (free ebook)
Harvard Business Review, 'Using Customer Data to Drive Marketing Decisions' (2023)

lecture_notes.introduction: Modern marketing is a software problem as much as a creative one. The average mid-market company now uses 90+ marketing tools, from analytics and CRM to email and automation, from SEO and social to creative and collaboration. Scott Brinker's annual Marketing Technology Landscape shows over 11,000 martech vendors as of 2024, up from 150 in 2011. Managing this sprawl is now a critical marketing capability, and marketers who cannot think architecturally about their tech stack end up with expensive, disconnected tools that produce bad data and worse decisions. The deeper challenge is data: marketing data lives across ad platforms, email platforms, e-commerce systems, web analytics, mobile analytics, CRM, customer service, product usage, and offline sources. Each system has its own identifier for the same customer, its own definition of metrics, and its own data schema. Without a unified view, marketers cannot answer basic questions like 'What is the LTV of customers acquired via Meta ads?' or 'Which product category do customers from SEO tend to buy from?' This is where Customer Data Platforms come in: they promise to unify customer data from all sources into a single profile with a consistent identity, which can then be used for segmentation, personalization, and analysis. But CDPs are expensive and complex to implement, and many brands fail to realize their value. Meanwhile, the deprecation of third-party cookies has forced brands to invest in first-party data strategies: capturing data directly from customers with consent, storing it in platforms they control, and activating it across channels. This session teaches you to think about marketing technology architecturally: what the major pieces do, how they connect, how to make build vs buy decisions, and how to prepare for a privacy-first future.
key_concepts: Marketing technology stack layers: data layer (analytics, data warehouses, CDPs), engagement layer (email, push, SMS, ads), content layer (CMS, DAM), operations layer (project management, collaboration), commerce layer (Shopify, Salesforce Commerce Cloud)

Customer Data Platform (CDP): unifies customer data from multiple sources into persistent profiles with consistent identity, activatable across marketing channels; leaders include Segment, mParticle, Tealium, Treasure Data, and Adobe

CRM vs CDP: CRM is built for sales teams to manage accounts and deals (Salesforce, HubSpot, Pipedrive); CDP is built for marketing teams to unify and activate customer data across channels; they complement rather than compete
Data Management Platform (DMP): historically used for ad targeting with third-party data; mostly obsolete in post-cookie world; functionality increasingly absorbed by CDPs and ad platforms

First-party data strategy: capturing data directly from customers through owned channels (website, app, email, purchases, surveys) with explicit consent; the only sustainable data strategy in a privacy-first world

Identity resolution: linking multiple identifiers (email, device ID, phone, cookie, customer ID) to a single customer profile; deterministic (based on login) is more accurate than probabilistic (based on behavior matching)

Data warehouse and analytics engineering: modern stacks use data warehouses (Snowflake, BigQuery, Databricks) as the source of truth, with marketing tools reading from and writing to the warehouse; enables custom analysis and AI
Reverse ETL: emerging category that syncs data from the warehouse back to operational tools (email platforms, ad platforms, CRM); Census, Hightouch, and RudderStack are leaders in this space

Marketing orchestration: platforms that coordinate multi-step, multi-channel customer journeys (Braze, Iterable, Customer.io); replace point solutions for email, push, and in-app with unified journey design

Privacy and consent management: OneTrust, TrustArc, and others manage cookie consent, data subject requests (GDPR, CCPA), and consent propagation to all downstream systems; non-compliance is expensive
lecture_notes.demonstration: Design a martech stack for a mid-sized D2C brand doing 25 million in revenue. Starting stack: Shopify Plus for commerce (2K/month), Klaviyo for email and SMS (1K/month), Meta Business Manager and Google Ads for paid (free, spend separate), Google Analytics 4 (free), Hotjar for session recording (99/month), Gorgias for customer service (400/month), Recharge for subscriptions (500/month). Problem: the marketing team cannot answer 'What is LTV by acquisition channel?' because data is scattered across Shopify (orders), Meta (ad spend and attribution), GA4 (web traffic), and Klaviyo (email engagement). Solution: add Segment as CDP (1.5K/month) to unify events and customer data; add Looker Studio or Mixpanel for analytics on top of Segment; optionally add a data warehouse (BigQuery, minimal cost) for deeper analysis. Result: unified customer profiles, cross-channel attribution, and LTV-by-channel reporting. Total stack cost grows from 4K/month to 6K/month, representing 0.3 percent of revenue, for dramatically better measurement and personalization capability. Discuss trade-offs: for smaller brands, Segment may be overkill; for larger brands, Segment alone is insufficient and they need a full data warehouse plus reverse ETL.
setup_requirements: Segment or RudderStack free account for CDP experimentation
Looker Studio for dashboard building (free)
Martech stack landscape reference (chiefmartec.com)
Spreadsheet for stack architecture diagram

review.value: Adding tools without thinking about how they integrate creates data chaos; plan the stack architecturally before buying tools. Every tool should have a clear role in the data flow, and overlapping tools should be consolidated

Third-party cookies are disappearing, and regulations are tightening; brands that invest in first-party data capture and activation will have sustainable advantages. Start treating email capture, account creation, and logged-in experiences as strategic priorities

review.key: CDP Is Not a Silver Bullet
review.value: CDPs promise unified data but require significant implementation effort and ongoing data modeling; most CDP implementations underperform expectations because teams underinvest in data quality. Treat CDP as a project, not a product

Buying point solutions is faster but leads to stack sprawl; building on data warehouse and reverse ETL is more flexible but requires engineering. Mid-sized brands typically should buy until they hit integration pain, then consolidate on a warehouse-centric architecture

class_schedule.topic: Digital Marketing Strategy, Budgeting, and Planning
learning_objectives: Build a comprehensive digital marketing strategy from business objectives through tactical execution
Allocate marketing budget across channels using marginal return analysis and strategic priorities
Forecast marketing performance using historical data, conversion assumptions, and scenario modeling
Present marketing strategy to executives with clear ROI projections and accountability metrics
readings: Chaffey & Ellis-Chadwick, Digital Marketing (8th ed.), Ch. 4 - Digital Marketing Strategy
Mark Ritson, 'Marketing Effectiveness' essays in Marketing Week
Les Binet and Peter Field, The Long and the Short of It: Balancing Short and Long-Term Marketing Strategies (IPA)
Harvard Business Review, 'The New Science of Customer Emotions' (2023)

lecture_notes.introduction: Strategy is where all the tactics you have learned in this course come together into a coherent plan that produces business results. Most digital marketers are strong tacticians and weak strategists; they can execute a Google Ads campaign or an email flow but struggle to connect those tactics to business objectives, allocate budgets across channels, and defend their plans to the CFO. This gap is what separates marketing managers from CMOs. A proper digital marketing strategy starts with business objectives (revenue, growth rate, profitability, market share) and cascades through marketing objectives (awareness, acquisition, retention, LTV), customer targets (segments and personas), value proposition (what makes this brand worth choosing), channel strategy (where to invest and why), creative strategy (what to say), measurement plan (how to know if it is working), and budget allocation (how much goes where). Each layer constrains and informs the next, and changes at higher levels cascade down. A common failure is building bottom-up from favorite tactics: the team that loves Google Ads pushes paid search regardless of whether it fits the business, while the team that loves content marketing starts a blog without thinking about how it connects to revenue. Good strategists think top-down. They also understand the long-term brand building versus short-term activation balance (Binet and Field's seminal research shows 60/40 brand vs activation is optimal for most businesses, yet most teams spend 80/20 in the wrong direction). They plan for test-and-learn budgets, not just proven channels. They build measurement frameworks that align with strategy, not just what is easy to measure. This session teaches you to think like a CMO: how to build strategy top-down, how to allocate budget based on marginal returns, how to forecast with confidence intervals, and how to present plans that get approved.
key_concepts: Strategy hierarchy: Business objectives cascade to Marketing objectives cascade to Target segments cascade to Positioning cascade to Channel strategy cascade to Creative strategy cascade to Measurement cascade to Budget; changes cascade downward

SMART marketing objectives: Specific (growth in X segment), Measurable (quantified KPIs), Achievable (realistic given resources), Relevant (connects to business goals), Time-bound (quarterly or annual targets)

Binet and Field framework: 60 percent of marketing budget should go to long-term brand building (reach, emotion, storytelling) and 40 percent to short-term activation (direct response, performance); most teams invert this and suffer long-term decline

Channel selection criteria: audience presence (is our customer there?), buying cycle fit (discovery vs consideration vs decision), economics (can we afford the CPA?), competitive intensity, team capability, strategic importance

Budget allocation approaches: percentage of revenue (simple, typical 6-12 percent for B2C, 5-8 percent for B2B), objective-and-task (estimate what is needed to hit objectives), competitive parity (match competitor spend), marginal ROI (shift budget to channels with highest incremental return)

Marketing Mix Modeling (MMM): statistical technique that measures the incremental contribution of each channel to sales, accounting for baseline, seasonality, and external factors; the gold standard for high-level budget allocation at scale
Test-and-learn budget: allocating 10-20 percent of budget to experimental channels and approaches; essential for finding growth, but often cut first in budget pressure

Forecasting methodology: top-down (target revenue divided by conversion rate and AOV gives required traffic) or bottom-up (historical channel performance scaled by planned spend); use both as reality checks
Scenario modeling: best case, base case, worst case projections under different assumptions; helps set realistic expectations and identify risks
Presenting to executives: lead with the business impact not the tactics, show ROI and payback periods, acknowledge uncertainty with ranges, connect to strategy, ask for clear go/no-go decision
lecture_notes.demonstration: Build a Year 1 digital marketing plan for a new D2C sustainable apparel brand with a 500K annual marketing budget and a goal of 3M in revenue. Top-down math: 3M revenue at 100 AOV = 30,000 orders; at 3 percent site conversion rate = 1M sessions needed; allowable CPA at 50 percent contribution margin and 20 percent marketing allocation = 20 CPA; customer LTV of 250 allows higher acquisition costs over time but Year 1 must approach break-even. Channel mix: Paid social (Meta and TikTok) 200K for rapid acquisition, Google Ads (brand and intent) 75K, SEO and content marketing 60K, email and retention 30K (tools plus content creation), influencer and PR 75K, creative production 50K, martech tools 10K. Forecast: paid social at 25 CPA drives 8,000 orders = 800K; Google Ads at 35 CPA drives 2,100 orders = 210K; SEO (slow ramp) drives 300K by Q4; email drives 25 percent of total revenue through retention; influencer drives brand awareness and assisted conversions worth 400K; total forecasted revenue 2.8M in base case, 3.5M in optimistic case. Present to CEO: 'We project 2.8M to 3.5M revenue with 500K marketing spend, representing 5.6-7x ROAS, with the biggest risks being paid social CPA inflation and slow SEO ramp. Approval requires accepting that Year 1 is breakeven and Year 2 benefits from lower paid dependency as SEO and email scale.'
setup_requirements: Marketing plan template (one-page strategy canvas)
Excel or Google Sheets for budget modeling
Historical benchmark data (Klaviyo, Shopify, WordStream reports)
PowerPoint or Keynote for executive presentation

review.value: Start with business objectives and cascade to tactics, not the other way around; teams that build strategy bottom-up from favorite channels end up with disconnected programs that do not roll up to business goals

Binet and Field's research demonstrates that 60 percent brand building and 40 percent activation outperforms pure performance marketing over 3+ years; do not let short-term pressure push you into 100 percent direct response. Invest in brand even when it is hard to measure

The right way to allocate budget is by marginal return: shift dollars to the channel where the next dollar returns the most, until all channels return equally. This is harder than percentage splits but produces better results
Forecasts are inherently uncertain; present ranges (best, base, worst case) with assumptions, not single-point predictions. Executives respect intellectual honesty more than false precision

class_schedule.topic: Ethics, Privacy, and the Future of Digital Marketing
learning_objectives: Explain the ethical dimensions of digital marketing including manipulation, dark patterns, and targeting vulnerable populations
Apply the major privacy regulations (GDPR, CCPA, new state laws) to digital marketing practices
Evaluate the impact of AI and automation on marketing roles, creativity, and measurement
Develop personal principles for ethical marketing practice in a changing regulatory environment
readings: Chaffey & Ellis-Chadwick, Digital Marketing (8th ed.), Ch. 3 - Ethical and legal issues section
Shoshana Zuboff, The Age of Surveillance Capitalism (selected chapters)
GDPR and CCPA official texts (summary versions)
Harvard Business Review, 'Is Marketing Ethics an Oxymoron?'

lecture_notes.introduction: Digital marketing operates in an increasingly contested ethical and regulatory space. The same tools that enable relevant, personalized customer experiences also enable manipulation, surveillance, and exploitation. The same data that helps brands serve customers better can be stolen, misused, or weaponized against the very people who generated it. Marketers face real ethical choices every day: dark patterns that trick users into subscriptions they did not want, lookalike targeting that can systematically exclude protected groups, pricing algorithms that charge desperate customers more, influencer content that disguises ads as authentic endorsement, and emotional manipulation of vulnerable audiences. The industry's track record on these issues is not great. Facebook's Cambridge Analytica scandal, Uber's Greyball program, Equifax's breach, and countless smaller incidents have eroded public trust in marketers' handling of data and advertising practices. Regulators have responded with GDPR in Europe (2018), CCPA in California (2020), and a growing patchwork of US state privacy laws. Apple and Google have tightened platform rules, and third-party cookies are finally being deprecated. The next five years will see dramatic changes: AI-generated content, agentic marketing systems that execute strategy autonomously, privacy-preserving measurement techniques, and regulatory enforcement on algorithmic discrimination. Marketers who treat ethics and privacy as obstacles to get around are setting themselves up for catastrophic failure. Marketers who treat them as constraints that drive innovation and earn trust will have durable advantages. This session is not about compliance checklists; it is about developing the judgment and principles that let you practice digital marketing in ways that are effective, legal, and worthy of the trust customers place in you. Your career will span decades of change, and your personal ethics will be tested repeatedly.
key_concepts: Privacy regulation landscape: GDPR (EU, 2018) with rights to access, delete, portability, and consent requirements; CCPA (California, 2020) granting similar rights to California residents; emerging state laws (Virginia, Colorado, Connecticut, Utah, Texas, and others); federal proposals pending
Consent management: the shift from implied consent (using site implies consent) to explicit consent (user must actively opt in); affects cookies, tracking, email, SMS, and profiling

Data subject rights: rights to access your data, correct errors, delete information (right to be forgotten), port data to competitors, object to automated decisions; implementing these rights requires infrastructure

Dark patterns: UX design tricks that manipulate users against their interest (pre-checked boxes, fake urgency, confirmshaming, hidden subscriptions); banned in some jurisdictions, reputation-damaging in all

Targeting ethics: lookalike modeling can inadvertently exclude protected groups (Facebook has faced lawsuits for housing and job ad discrimination); algorithmic fairness is an active area of research and regulation

Vulnerable populations: special care is required when marketing to children (COPPA), elderly, addicts, debt-stressed consumers, or people with mental health issues; exploitation is both unethical and increasingly illegal
Transparency in AI: as AI generates content, makes recommendations, and runs campaigns, customers deserve to know; the EU AI Act and other regulations are defining disclosure requirements

Environmental impact of digital marketing: programmatic advertising, video streaming, and data center operations consume significant energy; 'green marketing' and carbon accounting are emerging concerns

The future of marketing roles: generative AI will automate much routine content production, leaving humans to focus on strategy, judgment, and creativity; marketers who embrace AI tools will outperform those who resist
Personal marketing ethics: developing principles you will not violate regardless of business pressure; these become your professional reputation over a career
lecture_notes.demonstration: Case study discussion: evaluate the ethics of a real scenario and develop principles. Scenario: A weight loss supplement brand asks you to run Meta ads targeting women 18-35 who have searched for 'how to lose weight fast' and shown interest in diet content. The product is untested, not FDA-approved, and relies on before/after images from paid models. The brand wants ad copy playing on body image insecurity ('Summer is coming, do not be the one in the cover-up') and offers a subscription where customers must call to cancel. Walk through the issues: (1) Targeting vulnerability: using search history for 'lose weight fast' to target women likely has desperate, insecure intent; is this legitimate targeting or exploitation? (2) Product claims: before/after models without disclosure, untested product, may violate FTC guidelines. (3) Dark patterns: subscription with phone-only cancellation is increasingly illegal (FTC Click to Cancel rule). (4) Creative exploitation: playing on body image insecurity harms mental health especially among young women. Discuss what a marketer should do: refuse the work entirely, push back on specific tactics, run modified campaigns, or comply with directives? What if this represents 30 percent of agency revenue? Develop personal principles that would help navigate this kind of decision in your career.
setup_requirements: GDPR and CCPA summary documents for reference
FTC guidance documents on advertising and endorsements
Examples of dark patterns (deceptive.design gallery)
Group discussion format with breakout rooms

review.value: Privacy regulations are tightening globally, and consumer expectations are shifting; marketers who treat privacy as a constraint to minimize are on the wrong side of history. Build privacy-respecting practices into your approach from the start

Tricks that boost short-term metrics destroy long-term trust; a customer who feels manipulated into signing up will not become a loyal advocate. Measure whether your tactics would embarrass you in a headline

Generative AI is transforming content production, campaign execution, and personalization at unprecedented speed; marketers who learn to work with AI tools will have significant advantages, while those who resist will be displaced

Your marketing career will span decades, and your reputation compounds; the ethical choices you make early define who colleagues trust and who they avoid. Develop principles that guide your judgment under pressure, and revisit them regularly

quizzes.topic: Digital Marketing Foundations, Customer Journeys, and SEO

questions.question: A skincare D2C brand has a website receiving 80,000 monthly visitors with a 1.5 percent conversion rate and 60 dollar average order value. The CMO wants to increase monthly revenue by 50,000 dollars. Analyze three strategic options: (a) increase traffic by 40 percent through SEO, (b) improve conversion rate to 2.2 percent through CRO, (c) increase AOV to 75 dollars through upsells. Calculate the revenue impact of each and recommend which option to prioritize based on effort, time to impact, and compound effects.
questions.answer:
Current monthly revenue: 80,000 x 0.015 x 60 = 72,000.

Option (a) Traffic increase of 40 percent: 80,000 x 1.4 = 112,000 visitors; 112,000 x 0.015 x 60 = 100,800. Revenue lift: 28,800 (not sufficient alone to meet 50K goal).

Option (b) Conversion rate improvement to 2.2 percent: 80,000 x 0.022 x 60 = 105,600. Revenue lift: 33,600 (also insufficient alone).

Option (c) AOV increase to 75: 80,000 x 0.015 x 75 = 90,000. Revenue lift: 18,000 (smallest single impact).

No single option hits 50K. Combined options: If we achieve (a) and (b) together: 112,000 x 0.022 x 60 = 147,840, lift of 75,840 (exceeds goal). If (b) and (c): 80,000 x 0.022 x 75 = 132,000, lift of 60,000.

Recommendation: Prioritize CRO (option b) first because (1) it compounds with every other channel (traffic from SEO, paid, email all benefit from higher CR); (2) it typically shows impact in 2-4 weeks versus 6-12 months for SEO; (3) it does not require additional budget like paid channels. Second priority: AOV via upsells and bundles, which is quick to implement. SEO investment runs in parallel for long-term compounding returns but cannot be the sole near-term solution.

rubric.key: Correct calculations for each option showing math work
Recognition that no single option meets the goal and combination analysis
Recommendation prioritizing CRO with sound reasoning about compound effects, time to impact, and cost

questions.question: Map the customer journey for a first-time buyer of an Allbirds sneaker (a direct-to-consumer sustainable footwear brand). Identify at least 6 touchpoints across the journey, label each stage (awareness, consideration, decision, retention, advocacy), and identify one potential failure point where the brand could lose the customer.
questions.answer:
Touchpoint map:
1. Awareness: Customer sees a sponsored post on Instagram from a sustainability-focused influencer mentioning Allbirds materials.
2. Awareness: Customer Googles 'Allbirds review' and reads independent reviews on Wirecutter or Reddit.
3. Consideration: Customer visits allbirds.com on mobile to browse styles; bounces because selection is confusing.
4. Consideration re-engagement: Receives retargeting ad on Facebook showing the specific shoe they viewed with customer testimonials.
5. Decision: Returns to site on desktop, selects size and color; hesitates at checkout because of 95 dollar price point.
6. Decision: Activates free shipping and 30-day return guarantee messaging, proceeds to checkout.
7. Retention: Receives order confirmation, shipping notification, and delivery day email with care tips.
8. Retention: After 2 weeks, receives email asking for a product review with photos.
9. Advocacy: Shares photo on Instagram tagging Allbirds, which the brand reposts, creating visibility to the customer's network.

Potential failure point: Step 3 on mobile, where site navigation is confusing and the customer bounces. Without retargeting infrastructure (cookies, pixel), this customer might be lost entirely. Fix: simpler mobile navigation, sticky product filters, and robust pixel-based retargeting to recapture bounced visitors.

rubric.key: Six or more distinct touchpoints identified across the journey
Correct stage labels applied to touchpoints
Identification of a specific failure point with proposed fix

questions.question: A new B2B SaaS company selling project management software is debating whether to invest in SEO or paid search first. Their budget is 10K/month, the target keyword 'project management software' has an estimated 25 dollar CPC and 100,000 monthly searches, and their website is new (Domain Authority of 8). The CEO wants results in 3 months. Recommend a strategy with specific tactical reasoning about why SEO alone, paid alone, or a hybrid approach makes sense.
questions.answer:
Recommendation: Hybrid with 80 percent paid search and 20 percent SEO, shifting over time.

Reasoning for initial paid focus:
- Time to impact: SEO typically takes 6-12 months for a new domain (DA 8) to rank for competitive terms; the CEO wants results in 3 months, making SEO alone inadequate.
- Target keyword difficulty: 'Project management software' is dominated by Asana, Monday, Jira, and other established players with DA 80+; a new site has essentially zero chance of ranking in top 20 organically within any reasonable timeframe.
- Paid search can capture high-intent traffic immediately: with 8K/month budget at 25 dollar CPC, buys 320 clicks/month to a highly qualified audience already searching for the solution.

Reasoning for SEO investment in parallel:
- SEO compounds over time and becomes a sustainable asset; waiting to start means waiting longer for organic traction.
- Start SEO with long-tail keywords: 'project management software for marketing teams', 'free project management software under 10 users', 'how to choose a project management tool'; these are easier to rank for and attract qualified prospects.
- Content marketing and topic clusters can drive signups within 6-12 months at zero marginal cost.

Tactical plan:
- Paid search (8K): Target high-intent long-tail keywords, not just head terms; bid for 'project management software for [industry]' at lower CPC than the head term. Build a dedicated landing page per segment. Optimize aggressively for conversion rate.
- SEO and content (2K): Publish 2 high-quality long-form pieces per month targeting low-competition long-tail keywords; build a topic cluster around a chosen vertical focus.
- After 6 months, if SEO shows traction, shift budget toward SEO (50/50 split). After 12 months, SEO should drive more traffic than paid at lower cost.

rubric.key: Correctly identifies the SEO timeline mismatch with the 3-month deadline
Recommends hybrid with paid search as the near-term driver
Tactical reasoning about long-tail keywords and compound SEO over time

quizzes.topic: Content Marketing, SEM, and Social Media Strategy

questions.question: A Google Ads campaign has the following performance: Ad Group A has 1,200 clicks at 1.50 CPC, Quality Score of 4, and 2 percent conversion rate. Ad Group B has 800 clicks at 2.20 CPC, Quality Score of 8, and 3.5 percent conversion rate. (a) Calculate the cost per acquisition (CPA) for each ad group. (b) Explain why Ad Group B is more profitable despite the higher CPC. (c) What would you do to improve Ad Group A's Quality Score?
questions.answer:
(a) Cost Per Acquisition calculations:
Ad Group A: 1,200 clicks x 1.50 CPC = 1,800 spend; 1,200 x 0.02 = 24 conversions; CPA = 1,800 / 24 = 75.
Ad Group B: 800 clicks x 2.20 CPC = 1,760 spend; 800 x 0.035 = 28 conversions; CPA = 1,760 / 28 = 62.86.

(b) Why Ad Group B is more profitable:
Even though Ad Group B has a 47 percent higher CPC, its Quality Score of 8 vs 4 indicates much better relevance alignment between keyword, ad copy, and landing page. This higher relevance drives a higher conversion rate (3.5 percent vs 2 percent), and the conversion rate difference more than compensates for the CPC difference. The math: A converts at 2 percent so costs 75/conversion; B converts at 3.5 percent so costs 62.86/conversion. Ad Group B generates 28 conversions vs 24, at lower CPA, showing that the higher CPC is a worthwhile investment for higher-intent traffic.

Importantly, Ad Group B's higher Quality Score also earns it better ad positions at lower actual CPC than the bid would suggest, because Ad Rank = Bid x Quality Score x other factors.

(c) Improving Ad Group A's Quality Score from 4:
- Check keyword-ad relevance: ad copy may not mention the keyword; rewrite ad headlines to include target keywords.
- Tighten ad group scope: if Ad Group A has 50 mixed keywords, split into 5 tightly themed groups of 10 keywords each, each with dedicated ads.
- Improve landing page relevance: ad leads to homepage instead of a targeted landing page; build dedicated landing pages matching the ad keyword.
- Check historical CTR: low CTR is the main Quality Score factor; better ad copy with strong offers and calls to action drives CTR.
- Add negative keywords to filter unrelated searches that dilute metrics.

rubric.key: Correct CPA calculations for both ad groups
Clear explanation of why higher conversion rate outweighs higher CPC
At least three specific Quality Score improvement tactics

questions.question: Design a topic cluster for a personal finance blog whose pillar topic is 'investing for beginners'. Provide the pillar page target keyword, at least 8 cluster topics with target keywords, and explain how internal linking should work between pillar and cluster pages.
questions.answer:
Pillar page target keyword: 'investing for beginners' (search volume typically 40K+/month, high difficulty but strategic value).
Pillar page content: comprehensive 5000-word guide covering fundamentals, linking to all cluster pages as expanded sections.

Cluster topics with target keywords:
1. 'How to open a brokerage account' (target: 'how to open brokerage account', 15K searches, medium difficulty)
2. 'Index funds vs ETFs' (target: 'index funds vs etfs', 25K searches, medium difficulty)
3. 'What is a Roth IRA' (target: 'roth ira explained', 30K searches, medium difficulty)
4. 'Dollar-cost averaging strategy' (target: 'dollar cost averaging', 20K searches, low-medium)
5. 'Stock market basics for beginners' (target: 'stock market basics', 18K searches, medium)
6. 'How much to invest as a beginner' (target: 'how much to start investing', 8K searches, low)
7. 'Best investment apps for beginners' (target: 'best investment apps beginners', 12K searches, low-medium)
8. 'Understanding investment risk' (target: 'investment risk explained', 10K searches, low)
9. 'Compound interest explained' (target: 'compound interest for beginners', 8K searches, low)
10. 'Diversification for new investors' (target: 'what is diversification in investing', 6K searches, low)

Internal linking structure:
- Pillar page links OUT to all 10 cluster pages with descriptive anchor text in relevant sections; for example, the pillar's 'Choose a Broker' section links to cluster page on opening a brokerage account.
- Each cluster page links BACK to the pillar page at least once (often in a 'For a complete guide to investing, see our beginner's guide' callout).
- Related cluster pages link to each other where topically relevant (e.g., 'Roth IRA' page links to 'How much to invest' page).
- This structure signals topical authority to Google: the pillar establishes breadth, clusters establish depth, and internal links distribute authority across all pages.

Result: Google understands the site is a topical authority on investing for beginners, individual cluster pages rank for their target long-tail keywords (easier than the head term), and the pillar page gradually earns enough backlinks and authority to rank for the competitive head term over 12-24 months.

rubric.key: Pillar keyword and comprehensive pillar page description
At least 8 cluster topics with specific target keywords and volume/difficulty notes
Correct internal linking structure with pillar-cluster and cluster-cluster relationships

questions.question: A D2C cosmetics brand currently posts once a week on Instagram and gets an average of 500 likes per post on an account with 50,000 followers. Calculate the engagement rate and diagnose whether this is healthy. Recommend three specific changes to improve organic social performance, explaining the reasoning for each.
questions.answer:
Engagement rate calculation:
Engagement Rate = (likes + comments + shares) / followers, or per reach for more accurate measurement.
Assuming likes only: 500 / 50,000 = 1 percent engagement rate.

Diagnosis: 1 percent engagement rate is below average for beauty/cosmetics accounts. Industry benchmarks from Rival IQ and Later show beauty brands typically achieve 1.5-3 percent engagement rates, with top-performing brands exceeding 5 percent. Additionally, the account is likely reaching fewer than 10 percent of followers organically because Instagram's algorithm shows content to people most likely to engage, and low engagement signals low relevance. This creates a downward spiral: low engagement -> less reach -> even lower engagement.

Three recommendations:

1. Post more frequently and test formats: Posting once per week is insufficient to build algorithmic momentum. Competitors posting 5-7 times per week (mixing feed, Reels, Stories, and occasionally carousel posts) receive more opportunities to catch algorithmic waves. Specifically, prioritize Reels (Instagram's algorithm favors Reels heavily in 2024) at 3-4 per week, with feed posts and Stories filling the rest. Reels typically reach 3-5x more people than feed posts for accounts of this size.

2. Shift from product shots to community and UGC: Low engagement usually indicates content that feels promotional. Beauty customers engage with tutorials, transformations, behind-the-scenes content, and real customer results far more than studio product shots. Repost customer content (with permission), create before/after tutorial videos, and show founders or employees using the products. Glossier built their brand almost entirely on this approach, with user-generated content as the primary creative strategy.

3. Drive conversations in comments: Instagram's algorithm heavily weights comments as engagement signals. Ask open-ended questions in captions ('What is your biggest skincare struggle?'), respond to every comment within 1 hour of posting (triggers algorithmic boost), and use calls to action that invite dialogue rather than just likes. Additionally, collaborate with micro-influencers to increase exposure to similar audiences.

Expected outcome with these changes: engagement rate should climb from 1 percent to 2.5-3.5 percent within 90 days, follower growth should accelerate, and the account will escape the low-engagement algorithmic trap.

rubric.key: Correct engagement rate calculation with benchmark comparison
Three specific, differentiated recommendations
Clear reasoning for each recommendation tied to algorithm mechanics

quizzes.topic: Paid Social, Email Automation, and Influencer Marketing

questions.question: A DTC apparel brand runs a Meta Ads campaign with the following results over 30 days: Spend 30,000 dollars; Impressions 2,400,000; Clicks 48,000; Link clicks to site 38,000; Purchases 960; Purchase revenue 72,000 dollars. Calculate and interpret: (a) CPM, (b) CTR, (c) CPC, (d) Conversion rate from link click, (e) CPA, (f) ROAS. Identify which metric is the biggest problem and recommend a fix.
questions.answer:
Calculations:

(a) CPM (Cost per 1000 impressions) = (30,000 / 2,400,000) x 1000 = 12.50. Interpretation: 12.50 CPM is reasonable for mid-tier Meta ads targeting.

(b) CTR (Click-through rate on impressions) = 48,000 / 2,400,000 = 2.0 percent. Interpretation: 2.0 percent CTR is above average for Meta feed ads (industry average 1-1.5 percent).

(c) CPC (Cost per click) = 30,000 / 48,000 = 0.625. Interpretation: 0.63 CPC is healthy for e-commerce.

(d) Conversion rate from link click = 960 / 38,000 = 2.53 percent. Interpretation: 2.53 percent site conversion rate is average for e-commerce.

(e) CPA (Cost per acquisition) = 30,000 / 960 = 31.25. Interpretation: the cost to acquire one customer is 31.25 via Meta ads.

(f) ROAS (Return on ad spend) = 72,000 / 30,000 = 2.4x. Interpretation: for every 1 dollar spent on ads, the brand generates 2.40 in revenue.

Biggest problem: ROAS of 2.4x is borderline for most apparel brands. With typical apparel gross margins of 50-60 percent, 2.40 revenue produces only 1.20-1.44 in gross profit, barely covering the 1 dollar of ad spend. After accounting for fulfillment, returns, customer service, and other costs, the campaign is likely breakeven or unprofitable at this ROAS.

The biggest leverage point is conversion rate: at 2.53 percent, if we could improve to 4 percent through landing page and checkout optimization, the math changes dramatically. At 4 percent CVR: 38,000 x 0.04 = 1,520 purchases at same AOV of 75 = 114,000 revenue, for ROAS of 3.8x, which is healthily profitable.

Alternative fix: Increase AOV. Current AOV is 72,000 / 960 = 75. Adding upsells, bundles, and cross-sells to reach 95 AOV would generate 91,200 at same volume, for 3.0x ROAS.

Recommended action: Run site audit to identify friction points, deploy CRO tests on checkout and product pages, and implement post-purchase upsells to lift AOV. Conversion rate improvement has the highest leverage because it multiplies through every other metric.

rubric.key: All six metric calculations correct with proper formulas
Correct interpretation of ROAS as the primary concern
Specific fix recommendations with reasoning

questions.question: Design an email automation flow for a subscription meal kit service that has just had a customer cancel their subscription. Describe the flow structure (timing and messaging of each email), explain the goal at each step, and estimate the recovery rate the flow might achieve.
questions.answer:
Win-back flow for cancelled meal kit subscribers:

Trigger: Customer cancels subscription (not pausing, actually cancelling).

Email 1 - Immediate (within 1 hour): 'Sorry to see you go, can you tell us why?'
Goal: Understand reason for churn via a one-click survey (reasons: too expensive, meals not appealing, too much food, life changes, competitor switch). Data collection for product improvement, plus setting the tone that the brand cares about the customer experience. No pitch, no discount, just a human-feeling request for feedback.
Expected action rate: 15-20 percent click through to survey.

Email 2 - Day 7: 'Here are 3 new menu options we think you will love'
Goal: Reengage the interest by showing what is new, addressing the 'meals got boring' concern that is common in meal kit churn. Content: images of 3 new dishes with chef stories, seasonal ingredients, or collaborations. Soft call to action to resubscribe at regular price.
Expected action rate: 3-5 percent click back to site.

Email 3 - Day 14: 'We miss you - here is 30 percent off your next 3 boxes'
Goal: Provide a compelling financial incentive to test the service again. Limited-time offer (expires in 7 days) creates urgency. The discount is aggressive enough to overcome price objections but limited enough to avoid training customers to cancel for discounts.
Expected action rate: 5-8 percent reactivation.

Email 4 - Day 30: 'Before you forget us completely...' (last chance email)
Goal: Final touchpoint before removing from active marketing. Short, human-feeling message acknowledging that the meal kit may not be the right fit right now but offering a path back if they change their mind. Include a lower-intensity subscription option (every other week instead of weekly) as an alternative for customers who found weekly too much.
Expected action rate: 1-2 percent reactivation.

Email 5 (optional) - Day 90: Seasonal reengagement tied to a special event.
Goal: Some customers leave and come back later when life circumstances change; a seasonal nudge catches them at the right moment.

Overall expected recovery: 8-12 percent of cancelled subscribers will reactivate through this flow, with the highest-value recoveries coming from the personalized feedback in email 1 and the discount offer in email 3. At a typical 400 lifetime value for a meal kit subscriber, recovering 10 percent of 500 cancellations per month produces 200,000 in recovered revenue annually from an automated flow that runs with zero ongoing labor after setup.

Key principles: do not lead with a discount (Email 1), sequence the messages so each builds on feedback from the last, and provide alternatives to cancellation (reduced frequency) not just the original offer.

rubric.key: At least 4 email steps with appropriate timing
Clear goal articulated for each email step
Reasonable recovery rate estimate with business impact calculation
Strategic principles (do not lead with discount, provide alternatives)

questions.question: A wellness brand is evaluating an influencer partnership with a fitness influencer who has 450,000 Instagram followers, 5.2 percent engagement rate, and charges 8,000 dollars for a sponsored post plus one Story. Estimate the reach and potential business impact. What questions should the brand ask to vet the influencer before signing the contract?
questions.answer:
Reach and impact estimation:

Organic reach: Instagram organic reach for accounts of this size is typically 20-35 percent of followers for sponsored content. Estimated organic post reach: 450,000 x 0.25 = 112,500 people. Story reach is typically 10-15 percent of followers = 45,000-67,500 people.

Engagement: 5.2 percent of reach (not followers, for sponsored content) = 112,500 x 0.052 = 5,850 engagements (likes, comments, saves) on the post.

Click-through to link: Instagram link clicks from sponsored posts average 0.5-1.5 percent of reach, so roughly 560-1,700 clicks to the brand site. Story link clicks typically perform better at 1-3 percent, so 450-2,000 clicks.

Conversions: Assume the brand's site converts at 2.5 percent for this traffic. Total clicks (estimated 1,000-3,700) x 0.025 = 25-92 conversions. At 80 AOV, that is 2,000-7,360 in direct revenue.

Direct ROI: On an 8,000 spend generating 2,000-7,360 in direct revenue, the direct ROAS is 0.25-0.92x, i.e., the partnership loses money on direct attribution alone.

However, direct ROI misses most of the value: (1) brand awareness impressions to 112,500+ people in target audience compound over time; (2) retargeting pool grows from people who visited but did not convert; (3) the brand can reuse the influencer's content in its own paid ads (often the best use of creator partnerships); (4) some conversions happen days later and are not attributed directly.

Full value justification likely requires assuming LTV of 250+ per customer (not just AOV), brand awareness value, and content reuse rights. With those factors, the partnership might produce 10K-20K in total value.

Vetting questions before signing:

1. What is the audience breakdown by country, age, gender, and interests? (Use HypeAuditor or Modash to verify.)
2. What percentage of followers are estimated to be real vs bots?
3. What is the recent engagement trend? (Declining engagement is a red flag.)
4. Can we see examples of previous brand partnerships and their performance data?
5. What are the usage rights? Can we reuse the content in paid ads and for how long?
6. What is the exclusivity? Will the influencer promote competitors within 30 days before or after our post?
7. What FTC disclosure language will they use?
8. What happens if the post does not meet expected performance?
9. Can they provide a unique trackable link and promo code for attribution?
10. What is their audience's engagement with similar products in our category?

rubric.key: Reasonable reach, engagement, and conversion estimates with math work
Recognition that direct ROI often loses but full value includes brand awareness and content reuse
At least 5 substantive vetting questions covering audience, performance history, rights, and attribution

questions.question: An e-commerce site wants to A/B test a new product page design. The current page has a 4 percent conversion rate, and they want to detect a minimum 10 percent relative lift (i.e., from 4 percent to 4.4 percent). Traffic to the page is 8,000 visitors per week. (a) Estimate the sample size needed per variant for 95 percent statistical significance and 80 percent power. (b) How long should they run the test? (c) What common mistakes should they avoid during the test?
questions.answer:
(a) Sample size calculation:
To detect a relative lift from 4 percent to 4.4 percent (absolute difference of 0.4 percent) at 95 percent significance and 80 percent power, a standard calculator returns approximately 31,000-40,000 visitors PER variant. Small relative lifts on small baseline conversion rates require large samples.

The exact formula for two-sample proportion test:
n = (Z_alpha/2 + Z_beta)^2 x (p1(1-p1) + p2(1-p2)) / (p1 - p2)^2
With Z_alpha/2 = 1.96, Z_beta = 0.84, p1 = 0.04, p2 = 0.044:
n approximately 39,445 per variant.

So approximately 40,000 visitors per variant, or 80,000 total.

(b) Test duration:
At 8,000 visitors per week total, with 50/50 traffic split, each variant gets 4,000 visitors per week. To reach 40,000 visitors per variant requires 10 weeks.

However, most CRO experts recommend running tests for at least 2 full business cycles to capture weekly variation, and ideally through at least one full month to capture monthly patterns. At 10 weeks, the test captures meaningful time variation.

(c) Common mistakes to avoid:

1. Peeking at results early: Do not stop the test when results 'look good' after 2 weeks. Early results are dominated by noise, not signal. Peeking and stopping based on interim positive results inflates false positive rate from 5 percent to as high as 30-40 percent.

2. Running multiple tests simultaneously on the same page: If two tests are running on the same product page, their effects interact and results become impossible to interpret.

3. Changing the test during the run: Do not add new variants or modify the variants mid-test.

4. Ignoring segments: The overall test result may hide heterogeneous effects. A variant might win +15 percent for new visitors but lose -5 percent for returning visitors.

5. Ignoring external events: Running a test during Black Friday or a PR event creates atypical traffic that skews results.

6. Calling winners based on statistical significance alone: A statistically significant 0.2 percent lift may not be practically significant.

7. Not accounting for novelty effect: A new design may temporarily lift engagement because it is new, not because it is better.

rubric.key: Correct sample size estimation with reasoning or formula
Correct duration calculation considering both sample size and business cycles
At least 4 specific common mistakes with brief explanations

questions.question: Explain the difference between last-click and data-driven attribution models using a concrete example. How might switching from last-click to data-driven change a marketing team's budget allocation decisions?
questions.answer:
Last-click attribution: 100 percent of credit for a conversion goes to the final click before the conversion. All earlier touchpoints receive zero credit regardless of their influence.

Data-driven attribution: uses machine learning to determine how much credit each touchpoint should receive based on patterns in actual conversion paths. Typically examines thousands of conversion paths and assigns fractional credit based on incremental contribution.

Concrete example: A customer's path to purchasing running shoes from Brooks.com:
1. Day 1: Sees a Brooks video ad on YouTube (impression, no click)
2. Day 3: Clicks a Brooks ad on Instagram (paid social click)
3. Day 7: Reads a review on Runner's World (organic referral)
4. Day 10: Searches 'brooks ghost 16 review' and clicks a Brooks organic result (organic search)
5. Day 14: Searches 'brooks ghost 16 buy' and clicks a Brooks Google Ad (paid search)
6. Day 14: Purchases

Last-click attribution: 100 percent credit to paid search. YouTube, paid social, organic referral, and organic search receive 0 credit.

Data-driven attribution: Might assign something like:
- YouTube ad (first impression): 15 percent
- Paid social click (first website visit, added to retargeting pool): 20 percent
- Runner's World referral (built trust through review): 10 percent
- Organic search click (high-consideration research): 20 percent
- Paid search click (final decision): 35 percent

Budget allocation implications:

Under last-click, the marketing team sees paid search as the 'hero' channel. They would likely increase paid search budget and cut YouTube and paid social. This is the classic mistake: the customer would never have clicked paid search without the awareness channels that came before. Cutting them eventually starves the funnel of new prospects.

Under data-driven, the team sees that YouTube and paid social are valuable upper-funnel channels that deserve continued investment, and that paid search is largely capturing demand already created elsewhere. Budget reallocation: increase YouTube and paid social, maintain paid search, and possibly reduce excessive paid search bids on branded terms.

Data-driven attribution has caveats: (1) requires enough conversion volume to train the model; (2) still cannot account for offline touchpoints; (3) credits measured touches only; (4) different platforms' data-driven models give different answers.

Best practice: use data-driven as the default for budget decisions but supplement with incrementality testing and Marketing Mix Modeling for strategic decisions.

rubric.key: Clear definitions of both attribution models
Concrete multi-touchpoint example showing credit distribution
Budget allocation implications and strategic reasoning
Acknowledgment of data-driven attribution limitations

questions.question: A fitness app has a 10 dollar CPI on Meta Ads, 30 percent of installs open the app more than once (D1 retention), 8 percent are still active on D30, and 3 percent convert to paid subscription at 60 dollars per year. The subscription retention is 50 percent year 1 to year 2. Calculate the LTV per install and determine if the 10 dollar CPI is profitable.
questions.answer:
LTV per install calculation:

Step 1: Probability of subscribing per install = 3 percent = 0.03

Step 2: Expected revenue per subscriber in year 1 = 60 dollars

Step 3: Expected revenue in year 2 = 60 x 50 percent retention = 30

Step 4: Assuming further retention decay at similar rate (year 2 to year 3 = 50 percent), year 3 revenue = 15; year 4 = 7.50; year 5 = 3.75.

Total subscriber lifetime revenue (undiscounted, 5 years): 60 + 30 + 15 + 7.50 + 3.75 = 116.25

With a more conservative assumption of 2 years average subscriber lifetime: 60 + 30 = 90

Step 5: LTV per install = probability of subscribing x subscriber lifetime value
= 0.03 x 90 (2-year conservative) = 2.70 per install
= 0.03 x 116.25 (5-year optimistic) = 3.49 per install

Step 6: Profitability assessment
CPI is 10 dollars, LTV per install is 2.70-3.49.
10 CPI far exceeds 3.49 LTV, meaning each install loses the business 6.50-7.30.

Is the 10 dollar CPI profitable? No, not even close. The app needs to either:
(a) Dramatically increase the install-to-subscription rate from 3 percent to at least 11 percent.
(b) Increase subscription price from 60 to 220 per year (probably not acceptable).
(c) Add additional revenue streams per user (in-app purchases, advertising).
(d) Reduce CPI to 3 dollars or below through better creative, targeting, and channel mix.
(e) Accept the loss as brand investment and assume LTV improves over time.

Realistic path forward: Work on improving the install-to-paid conversion rate first. A 30 percent D1 retention and 8 percent D30 retention suggest the app has an onboarding problem and a habit formation problem. Fixing these will increase the subscription rate because retained users subscribe.

Benchmark: most successful freemium apps target LTV:CAC ratios of 3:1 or better. To justify 10 dollar CPI, the app needs LTV of 30+, which requires a 10 percent install-to-paid conversion rate at 90 subscriber LTV.

Immediate action: do not scale spending until the unit economics work. Either fix the product-market fit and funnel metrics, or reduce CPI by an order of magnitude.

rubric.key: Correct LTV calculation with multi-year subscriber revenue
Clear profitability assessment showing current economics lose money
Realistic recommendations for improving either LTV or CPI

questions.question: You are the new head of marketing at a 15M revenue D2C skincare brand. The previous marketing lead allocated 90 percent of the 2M marketing budget to paid social (Meta primarily). The CEO is concerned about over-reliance on one channel and asks for your reallocation plan. Propose a new channel mix with specific percentages and explain the reasoning using the Binet and Field framework.
questions.answer:
Diagnosis of current state:

The previous allocation of 90 percent to paid social is strategically fragile:
1. Single platform risk: iOS 14 showed how a single platform change can crater performance.
2. No upper funnel: paid social on cold audiences is bottom-funnel direct response.
3. Missing retention value: no email investment means each acquired customer is treated as a one-time transaction.
4. No compounding assets: paid spend generates no compounding value.

Binet and Field framework recommends 60 percent long-term brand building and 40 percent short-term activation. The current 10 percent long / 90 percent short is inverted.

Proposed reallocation (Year 1, 2M budget):

Brand and long-term (60 percent = 1.2M):
- Content marketing and SEO: 300K (hire content lead, produce 4 long-form pieces/month, build topic clusters)
- YouTube and video content: 250K (founder interviews, product education, customer stories)
- PR and earned media: 150K (PR agency retainer, press outreach)
- Brand campaigns on paid social: 300K (upper-funnel video ads designed for reach and awareness)
- Influencer and creator partnerships: 200K (ongoing relationships with 10-15 micro-influencers)

Activation and short-term (40 percent = 800K):
- Paid social direct response (retargeting and high-intent cold): 400K (cut from 1.8M)
- Paid search (brand defense plus high-intent category terms): 150K
- Retail media and affiliate: 50K
- Email and SMS (tools, content, optimization): 150K (aggressive investment)
- CRO and testing: 50K

Rationale:

- 60/40 split aligns with Binet and Field research.
- Cutting paid social from 1.8M to 400K + 300K brand = 700K still maintains significant Meta presence.
- Email investment is critical: D2C brands with mature email programs generate 30-40 percent of revenue from email.
- SEO and content are slow but compound.
- YouTube and PR build brand awareness that paid social cannot.

Risks and mitigation:

- Year 1 may show lower ROAS than the previous all-paid-social model because the investments in SEO, content, and brand take time to pay off; communicate this clearly to the CEO and board.
- Monitor leading indicators: email list growth, organic traffic, branded search volume, social following growth, content engagement.
- Revisit allocation quarterly; shift budget toward whatever is producing strongest marginal returns.
- Have a fallback plan if paid social suddenly stops working.

rubric.key: Correct diagnosis of risks in the current allocation
Proposed mix with specific percentages summing to 100 percent and aligned with Binet and Field 60/40 split
Clear reasoning for each channel allocation with strategic justification
Acknowledgment of risks and year-over-year evolution

questions.question: A fashion retailer is planning to implement a Customer Data Platform (CDP). Currently, their customer data lives in Shopify (purchases), Klaviyo (email engagement), Meta (ads), GA4 (web analytics), and Gorgias (support tickets). Explain what specific capabilities a CDP would add, what implementation challenges they should expect, and what they should measure to know if the CDP is delivering value.
questions.answer:
Current state problems:

With customer data scattered across five systems, the retailer cannot answer questions like:
- What is the LTV of customers acquired from Instagram vs Google?
- Which customers who abandoned carts also engaged with email marketing?
- How do support interactions correlate with churn?

Capabilities a CDP adds:

1. Unified customer profiles: A single customer record that combines all touchpoints across all systems, identified by email, phone, device ID, or login.

2. Identity resolution: deterministic matching (when a customer logs in) and probabilistic matching (when they do not) to connect anonymous sessions to known customers.

3. Real-time segmentation: Dynamic audience segments that update instantly as customer behavior changes.

4. Cross-channel activation: Push segments to email platforms, ad platforms, personalization engines, and customer service tools.

5. First-party data asset: Captures and stores data the brand owns, independent of third-party cookies and platform restrictions.

Implementation challenges:

1. Data quality is harder than expected: customer emails may be misspelled, purchases may have duplicate customer records. Cleaning and validating data consumes 40-60 percent of typical CDP implementation effort.

2. Identity resolution is imperfect: probabilistic matching makes mistakes. Expect 70-85 percent identity resolution at best.

3. Integration work: Each data source needs a connector. Budget 3-6 months for integration.

4. Governance and privacy: CDPs concentrate sensitive customer data in one place, creating a high-value target for breaches.

5. Ongoing data modeling: The CDP does not magically decide what 'LTV' or 'churn' means.

6. Organizational alignment: If marketing and engineering do not agree on data definitions and priorities, the CDP becomes shelfware.

Success metrics:

1. Data completeness: percentage of known customers with unified profiles; target 70-80 percent within 6 months.

2. Activation velocity: time to create and launch a new segment-based campaign should drop from days to minutes.

3. Campaign performance lift: measure conversion rate, open rate, and revenue per recipient for CDP-powered campaigns; target 20-30 percent lift in first year.

4. Personalization reach: percentage of customer touchpoints that use personalized content.

5. Business metrics: LTV growth, retention rate, repeat purchase rate.

6. Team efficiency: marketing team hours spent on data pulls and manual analysis.

A CDP is not a quick win; it is a 12-24 month transformation project.

rubric.key: Clear explanation of current data silo problems
At least 4 specific CDP capabilities with business value
Realistic implementation challenges including data quality and identity resolution
Measurable success metrics tied to business outcomes

questions.question: A D2C brand notices that their Meta Ads performance has been declining: CPM increased 25 percent in the last 60 days, CTR dropped from 2.1 percent to 1.4 percent, and ROAS fell from 3.5x to 2.1x. Diagnose the likely causes and propose a three-part recovery plan.
questions.answer:
Likely causes of performance decline:

1. Creative fatigue: the most common cause. The same creative has been running for too long. Check average frequency - if above 5-6 impressions per user per week, fatigue is likely.

2. Audience saturation: the addressable audience has been exhausted.

3. Seasonal effects: CPMs and CPCs cycle seasonally.

4. Competitive escalation: competitors entering the market drives auction prices up.

5. Attribution changes: if the iOS 14.5 IDFA impact was delayed, measured conversions may be under-reported.

6. Landing page or site issues: if site performance degraded, conversion rate drops.

7. Product or offer fatigue: the same offer that worked 6 months ago may be stale.

Recovery plan (three parts):

Part 1 - Creative refresh (immediate, 0-2 weeks):
- Audit creative performance: identify which ads have been running longest and have highest frequency; pause them.
- Commission new creative variations: 5-10 new concepts across different angles (social proof, benefits, lifestyle, founder story, UGC).
- Test rapidly: run all new creatives with a small budget to identify winners within 7 days.
- Expand creative formats: if primarily running static images, add Reels and video.

Part 2 - Audience expansion and refresh (2-4 weeks):
- Create new audiences: test interest-based audiences not previously used, broad targeting, and custom audiences based on CRM segments.
- Refresh lookalikes: build new lookalikes from recent high-value customers.
- Test broader targeting: Meta's algorithm has improved at finding customers within very broad audiences.
- Exclude recent converters to avoid wasting spend.

Part 3 - Measurement and site fixes (parallel):
- Verify attribution: compare reported Meta conversions to Shopify revenue to check for attribution loss.
- Audit site performance: check Core Web Vitals, page load time, mobile experience, checkout flow.
- Run CRO experiments on landing pages.
- Consider incrementality testing: use a geo holdout to measure true Meta contribution.

Expected outcome: creative refresh typically recovers 30-50 percent of lost performance within 4 weeks; audience refresh adds another 15-25 percent.

rubric.key: At least 4 likely causes with specific diagnostic approaches
Three-part recovery plan with concrete tactics
Expected outcome estimates and fallback plan if tactics fail

major_assignments.title: Digital Marketing Audit and Strategic Recommendations
major_assignments.description: Conduct a comprehensive digital marketing audit of a real brand of your choosing (D2C, SaaS, or service business with meaningful digital presence). Analyze their current state across SEO, paid search, paid social, content marketing, email, and social media. Identify strengths, gaps, and opportunities. Produce strategic recommendations prioritized by impact and effort, backed by data.
objectives: Apply audit frameworks learned in class to a real brand in a systematic way

Develop prioritized recommendations that balance short-term wins with long-term investments
Present findings and recommendations in a professional format suitable for a CMO audience
deliverables: Audit report (15-20 pages) with sections for each channel analyzed (SEO, paid search, paid social, content, email, organic social)
Competitive benchmarking against 3 direct competitors using SimilarWeb, Ahrefs, or SEMrush
Data exhibits (screenshots, charts, exported data) supporting all findings
Executive summary (1 page) with key findings and top 5 recommendations
Prioritized recommendations matrix ranked by estimated impact and effort
Presentation deck (10-15 slides) summarizing the audit for executive stakeholders

criteria: Uses appropriate tools (Ahrefs, SEMrush, SimilarWeb, Meta Ad Library) to gather data for analysis

Competitive benchmarking provides meaningful comparison data
Recommendations are specific and actionable, not generic advice

Presentation deck follows professional design principles with minimal text per slide

Analysis identifies root causes, not just symptoms

Professional writing quality throughout with attention to grammar and clarity

Professional presentation suitable for executive audience

major_assignments.title: Integrated Digital Marketing Campaign
major_assignments.description: Design a complete integrated digital marketing campaign for a hypothetical or real product launch, spanning 90 days from pre-launch through launch through post-launch optimization. The campaign must include coordinated efforts across at least five channels (SEO, paid search, paid social, content, email), with clear messaging, creative concepts, measurement plan, and budget allocation.
objectives: Integrate multiple digital marketing channels into a coordinated campaign with consistent messaging
Develop realistic budget allocation with justified reasoning
Design creative concepts appropriate for each channel's native format and audience mindset
Build a measurement framework that enables optimization during the campaign
deliverables: Campaign brief (3-5 pages) with objectives, target audience, key messages, success metrics, and budget
Channel plan: detailed tactics for each of 5+ channels with specific creative concepts, targeting, and budget
Creative samples: mockups of at least 3 ad variations per channel (can be Canva mockups)
90-day campaign timeline showing pre-launch, launch, and post-launch phases with key activities
Measurement plan: KPIs, attribution model, reporting cadence, and optimization triggers
Forecast model: spreadsheet showing expected performance (impressions, clicks, conversions, revenue) with assumptions

Assumptions are clearly stated and defended

major_assignments.title: Digital Marketing Strategy Final Project
major_assignments.description: Develop a complete digital marketing strategy for a real business over a 12-month horizon. Choose a business (D2C brand, SaaS company, local service business, or nonprofit) and build a strategy from business objectives through tactical execution through measurement. This is the capstone project synthesizing everything learned in the course.
objectives: Build a top-down digital marketing strategy grounded in business objectives

course_policies.attendance: Attendance is expected for all lectures. Class discussions and workshop activities cannot be made up, so more than 3 unexcused absences may result in a reduced participation grade.
course_policies.late_submissions: Late assignments lose 10 percent per day. No submissions accepted more than 5 days late. Major projects with extenuating circumstances should be discussed with the instructor before the deadline, not after.
course_policies.collaboration: Discussion of strategies and approaches is encouraged. All written analysis, creative concepts, and strategic recommendations must be individual work. Group discussion must be documented if it influenced your submission.
course_policies.academic_integrity: All submitted work must be your own. AI tools may be used for brainstorming and drafting but final work must be substantially your own analysis and writing, with AI use disclosed. Fabricating data, plagiarism, and unauthorized collaboration constitute academic dishonesty.
course_policies.technology_requirements: Students need access to Google Analytics Demo account, Meta Ads Manager (requires payment method even for demo campaigns), Google Ads, Canva, and at least one SEO tool (Ahrefs or SEMrush free trials work). Some premium tools have educational discounts.
